Also where streets and roads have not been visually updated or enhanced through greening and more updated street design. There are opportunities to tackle speeding traffic to make roads child-friendly. To also look at HGVs passing through some roads that are not suited for large vehicles. It shouldn’t be that an accident needs to happen for changes. Let’s review this for Bexhill and all East Sussex areas. So whether by car, bike, or as a pedestrian we have better safer travel.

Schools
There a few issues affecting local schools. SEND provision is a worry. While the government has a review it may not be adequate, with concerns from staff and schools. As per our news story (from June 2025), many children are not getting the special education needs and support they need. We Greens believe the most vulnerable need to be protected. Moreover all our children need a safe thriving environment that enables them to enjoy learning.

Local NHS Provision & Care
Local NHS services and GP services (especially) are under pressure. Super-surgeries are planned so as to offer more appointments and cater for treatments and check-ups that could be done locally. However, if they aren’t accessible then local surgeries in walking or easy transport reach must stay. I would prefer some town-centre provision. So too when thinking of our elderly citizens or those in care their care needs should be met. I believe wellbeing and community is one aspect of this, but the social care aspect needs a review to help support those in need.

Environment
There have been many pollution incidents recently. The Southern Water sewage incidents in Bexhill. The bio-beads spillage that affected Rye and Camber. The container spills that caused large sections of our beaches, including Bexhill, to be covered in debris. This stems from treating our coastal areas and waters as not living spaces for wildlife and us. I will be working with others to change perceptions, to make areas wildlife protected. Wildlife and residents shouldn’t have to suffer due to disregard to nature and our environment.

I'm honoured to be running to represent the Heathfield & Mayfield Division on East Sussex County Council.
As a District Councillor I have a proven track record of listening to concerns of local residents and delivering for them. 
My other role as a funeral celebrant requires excellent communication and organisational skills, deep empathy and an ability to bring people together, all important skills for a good councillor.
Unlike other political parties, Greens are not told what to do and how to vote by their national offices. I will be free to be a strong and effective voice for local concerns.

My priorities include:
- Improving SEND provision for children and supporting parents and teachers
- Public ownership and control of Highways to improve road conditions
- Better road safety and lower speed limits in villages and near schools
- Promote active travel with more cycleways on rural and urban roads
- Take action on fly tipping, litter and anti-social behaviour
- Protect our countryside and the health of our rivers by holding water companies and big polluters to account

I’ve lived here my whole life and I am studying Politics and International Relations at SOAS, University of London, where I will be graduating this year. I was raised in a family that taught me the values of giving back to my community. I’ve already see the difference that Enfield Labour has made locally and want to build upon those achievements.

As a Labour Councillor, I aim to make Bush Hill Park a safer, cleaner neighbourhood. I am a member of my university’s women’s rugby team and know the importance of having a diverse range of sports programs available to young people, investing into our green spaces and upgrading playground equipment for families. As a flutist,  I am also passionate about organising art and music classes in our community centres, giving residents of all ages the chance to explore creative hobbies. Particularly for young people, I want to run workshops connecting them to companies offering apprenticeships and work experience, alongside public speaking classes to helps build their confidence for the world of work.

With your support, I will echo the voices of residents and tackle the issues that matters most to you. I will work alongside you to make our neighbourhood safer, invest in the next generation and ensure Bush Hill Park is a place that all can proudly call home.

I have lived  Locally  for over six years. I earned an IT Diploma at Enfield College of Haringey and later studied law at the University of Law. My time volunteering with youth at Albany Church Restore Community Youth Club inspired my commitment to community building.

"If elected, I will focus on three priorities: better access to social services, improved local infrastructure, and safer roads and transport. My goal is a Jubilee that works for everyone—especially our elderly residents and children."

Arben Neza is 45 years old and lives in Edmonton with his partner, where they are bringing up their young family.

He has run his own successful construction company, in Edmonton, for over 16 years. He attended London Metropolitan University obtaining a degree in International relations and British politics. Arben is active in his local community and is driven by a desire to help and support others and to build and safeguard a better future for all.

Keiron was, by his own admission, politically homeless until 2021, having never had an active role in politics or association with any party. He joined Reform UK in 2021 after finding so many similarities with his own political beliefs that he decided to stand himself.

Since then Keiron has been a regular & visible presence in Castle Point, and is encouraged by the growing local support.

Keiron has worked within sales in different industries ever since he turned 21, since his first-ever job at Burger King in Southend, followed by 2 summers spent at Adventure Island! During 2020 he was to be found delivering pizzas most evenings in Rayleigh when everything else shut down.

Far from being all work and no play, Keiron is an avid F1, Indycar and football fan & has recently become a regular fan watching the Chelmsford Chieftains ice hockey team. 

Keiron is happy to answer any queries you have at any time, so please do not hesitate to contact him. And he is committed to serving Castle Point to the very best of his ability.

As a doctor with 18 years of experience, including 8 years as an A&E consultant, I have dedicated my career to standing up for patients and improving healthcare services. I lead and contributed to transforming children’s A&E at one of the Essex Hospitals.

I currently lead the medical examiner service at a major London hospital, ensuring the highest standards of care and accountability.

Throughout my career, I worked on the frontline day and night, including during the COVID-19 pandemic. I have seen firsthand the challenges facing our NHS. That is why I am committed to fighting for my residents to receive the right treatment in the right place. I will campaign tirelessly against unacceptable waiting times and the crisis of corridor care that leaves patients without dignity and proper support.

As a Conservative, I believe in strong leadership, efficiency, and delivering real improvements for our healthcare system and our community.

My wife and I are proud to raise our two children in Chelmsford, where they attend local schools, and I am passionate about ensuring a better future for families like ours.

I will work relentlessly to ensure our healthcare system delivers the timely, high-quality care our residents deserve. Together, we can build a healthier, stronger community.

I’m standing for Reform UK in the Thaxted Division because too many local people feel they are being taken for granted.  Decisions are too often made without enough regard for the people who actually live here, pay for services, and deal with the consequences.  Residents are expected to accept overdevelopment, stretched infrastructure, declining standards and weak accountability as if that is simply the price of modern life.  It isn’t.  Our area deserves representatives who will say no when no is the right answer, who will challenge poor decisions, and who will put local people first.  The county election for Thaxted Division is on 7 May 2026.

I’ve lived near Thaxted since 2009 and I’ve spent the last twelve years volunteering locally.  I served as Chair of Thaxted Primary PTA for four years, helping raise funds for new laptops for the school, and I was on the executive committee for six years.  I have been Chairman of 1st Thaxted/Carver Scouts since 2018, having previously served as a Beaver and Cub Leader.  During that time, the group has more than doubled in size and fundraised to buy a van.  I also coached youth rugby at Saffron Walden RFC from 2018 to 2024.  I’m not standing because I want to become a politician.  I’m standing because I’m tired of seeing communities asked to accept more pressure, more building and less voice.

Professionally, I’ve spent around thirty years working in financial markets and business, including senior leadership roles in the City.  That experience has taught me the importance of accountability, sound decision-making, getting value for money and not passing problems down the road — qualities I believe are badly needed in local government.

My priorities are clear.  First, education and youth provision: better support, more activities and safe places for young people to meet, learn and thrive.  Second, infrastructure-first development: no more rubber-stamping growth before roads, schools, GP access and basic services are ready.  Development must serve the community, not the other way round.  Third, stronger support and connection for older and more isolated residents, because a decent community does not leave people behind once services become harder to reach.

For too long, local representation has too often felt passive, cautious and willing to manage decline instead of confronting it.  I believe people want representatives who are visible, prepared to speak plainly and willing to push back when their community is getting a bad deal.  Thaxted and the surrounding villages do not need more excuses.  They need stronger representation.

On immigration, I believe in a welcoming country, but compassion must be matched by capacity and by the rule of law.  We need a system that is fair, controlled and properly enforced, with clear rules, quicker decisions and proper funding for local services.  Those who come here legally and contribute should be welcomed.  But those who are here illegally, or who have no legal right to remain, should be removed.  Local communities should not be left to deal with the consequences of weak borders, slow decisions and overstretched services.  That is not intolerance.  It is common sense and responsible government.

The towns and villages in Thaxted Division cannot keep being asked to accept more housing, more strain on roads and services, and less say over what happens locally.  That is not sustainable and it is not fair.  I’m standing to push back, to demand better planning and stronger infrastructure, and to make sure local people are heard before decisions are imposed on them.

Kasi is a Portsmouth native, but has lived in Fareham since 2012, where she has been raising her family with her husband. She is passionate about social justice and taking care of the environment, regularly doing litter picks on the beach with her family.

Aubrey was born in Widnes and has lived in the town centre for the majority of their life. She is incredibly passionate about the local community and will do all she can to serve Widnes' residents. Whether that is by improving youth programmes to target antisocial behaviour, supporting and protecting local green spaces, or listening to the needs and desires of local businesses. Aubrey understands that many residents feel that they aren't being heard or seen by their Council, and they aim to change that.

Vote Green for hope in a better future

- For a borough where residents are listened to

- Local policies for a greener, sustainable future

- Cleaner air and more affordable housing

- Repairs and improvements for residents, not demolitions for the benefit of billionaire investors

- An end to H&F's pension fund investing in the Israeli arms industry and all weapons factories

Vote Green for hope in a better future

For a borough where residents are listened to

Local policies for a greener, sustainable future

Cleaner air and more affordable housing

Repairs and improvements for residents, not demolitions for the benefit of billionaire investors

An end to H&F's pension fund investing in the Israeli arms industry and all weapons factories

We will work to build a fair and healthy community. Residents concerns will be our priority and we will push for democratic ways to involve residents in decisions that affect them. I am proud to live in this local area and want a better standard of living for all. We can help to achieve this through helping to make our borough energy secure with solar panels and heat pumps. This will help bring down our bills and make life more stable, help clean the air we breath and tackle the causes of the climate crisis.

We also aim to enrich our public spaces, the places where we connect: let's make clean green travel journeys the easiest choice to make, let's provide free-to-access activities like table tennis, chess, and outdoor gyms in our green spaces, let's have play streets.

We can get the basics right and aim for a positive vision of our local area. We look forward to serving our community.

I'm standing as a Green councillor because I believe that the residents deserve better. We deserve well funded local services and a clean environment. We deserve to be listened to and our needs and safety to be prioritised over the profits of billionaire investors.

The last few years, I've coordinated a mutual aid group and worked with charities to provide asylum seekers with a real community and essential services - healthy food, clothes, advice and exercise. Meanwhile, our government demonised immigrants and refugees instead of addressing the pressing concerns of our communities - rising rents, housing falling into disrepair, loss of public spaces and youth services.

As a councillor, I would work to amplify yours and your neighbours' voices. I want the council to work for the residents - the decision making process should be transparent and participatory.

My priorities are investing in affordable housing, youth services and community spaces, public transport as well as meaningful local climate action.

I’m Rafela and I’ve lived in Shepherd’s Bush since 1992. I love it here: the Market, the Theatre, the independent shops and restaurants, the incredible spirit and diversity of our community. But I’m not blind to our problems. For example, as a Geography teacher, I’m very aware of the impacts of climate change, globally and locally; I also teach about the deep and increasing inequality in UK society - and the reasons for it (spoiler: it’s not immigration!).  I have been very active volunteering in this community and I can’t wait to work with residents for positive changes in Shepherd’s Bush.

I am dedicated to working towards ensuring habitable and well-maintained housing across the ward, as Hammersmith & Fulham, including White City, has one of the worst track records for the maintenance of council housing in the country.

Beyond that, I am dedicated to providing a voice for everyone in the ward and finding solutions for problems that work for directly for residents.

Living in Bridgemary and running a small family business gives me a chance to communicate with everyone and learn every day. I have helped my community in tough times by cooking for families and vulnerable members of the community. I am always keen to help all residents where I can. I understand the issues in our local area, am passionate and aim to work hard for Bridgemary North helping residents with my fresh ideas.

Prad was one of the leading voices at the Planning Appeal against the County Council’s backing of Cemex’s plan for gravel extraction at Hamble Airfield and 15 years of additional heavy traffic on Hamble Lane. He is continuing his opposition with the support of the community campaign group, Hamble Parish Council and the area’s County Councillor, Cllr Keith House. Prad is backing concerns in Netley from residents worried by speeding traffic through the village and helped secure a reduced speed limit at Grange Road near the Primary School entrance. He is Cabinet Member for Children’s Services at the Borough Council.

I am a transplant surgeon with over 35 years’ experience in the NHS, and have lived in the New Forest for over 10 years. My time is now divided between helping out in transplant units in the UK, and working overseas in conflict zones such as Gaza. I have previously led negotiations on behalf of the BMA for both junior and senior doctors, successfully introducing new contracts for both groups. As a councillor, I would be committed to working hard to improve local services and encourage implementation of Green policies, such as improving public transport. I will actively seek the views of all those I represent throughout my tenure, not only prior to election.

We are not career politicians and **we are not bound by party positions or internal discipline**. We live in the area we seek to represent, and we care deeply about its future. We became involved through everyday local issues - changes to streets, transport and parking - where many residents felt decisions were being made without our voices being heard or properly considered.

Like others locally, we engaged with meetings and consultations, but too often it felt as though outcomes were decided in advance. Over time, it became clear that borough-wide priorities and party lines were frequently taking precedence over what works best for Alexandra Park ward.

We believe **our neighbourhood deserves councillors whose first responsibility is to their community**, not to party structures. That is why we are standing as Independents - free to question proposals, challenge poor decisions, and **put Alexandra Park residents first**.

We are standing as independent candidates focused on everyday issues in our area:

- Protect local parking against further expansion of CPZs
- Support balanced transport policies that work for drivers, pedestrians, and cyclists alike
- Safer streets, repaired roads and pavements, and better lighting
- Push for step-free access at Alexandra Palace Station
- Protect Alexandra Park Library from cuts
- Protect Pinkham Way green space
- No to building over Muswell Hill car parks
- Ensure transparency and accountability in the Council’s decision-making processes

**Realistic promise**: We can’t fix it all but we will speak up for residents, ask difficult questions, challenge decisions that do not stand up to scrutiny, and make the voices of our neighbourhood heard where it matters.

- No wider political agenda
- No party whip
- Just local people standing up for the community

As a long-time resident of Alexandra Park, this community means the world to me, and my family.

My genuine connection to our local area means a first hand experience/understanding of local issues, and a vested interest to ensure resident-informed solutions and bold actions on local issues such as; traffic, fly tipping in certain corners, theft and anti-social behaviour, housing disrepair which affects our neighbours in council properties, ensuring that no tenant lives in damp and mould infested property, making our pavements safer especially for our elderly, installing lightening in our parks to make them safer for all residents especially women and young people during the dark winter months, continue our investment in local playgrounds for young families, securing step free access for Alexandra Palace Station for all but especially parents like me with pushchairs and our disabled neighbours, continue the protection of vital community assets such as our Alexandra Park library, ensuring that the library remains open and expanding activities and restoring full opening hours, protecting of our green spaces including green corridors like Pinkham Way.
It is also vital to maintain and improve the quality of services that are already being delivered well, such as our bin collections.

Employment and skills support for our young people, especially those not in education or employment (NEETS), as well as struggling families still facing difficulties due to the devastating impacts of Covid Pandemic and the austerity imposed by the Conservatives/Liberal Democrats coalition government.

At work in a London Local Authority, I stand up to developers and their contractors. As London changes and high rises shoot up, it’s vital that developers give back to the communities they profit from. This could be in the form of employment and skills and support for local services et cetera.

I’d be honoured to represent you, to ensure that those of us who live in this wonderful community are properly consulted to shape decisions that affects us and our community. I’ll be available not just during elections, and I’ll listen and ensure that the council responds to our community’s issues.

Together, let’s build a safer, cleaner and fairer Alexandra Park!

Brij has been involved in the Haringey community for over 20 years, including serving as a parent governor and deputy chair of governors at a local school. That experience has given him direct insight into the pressures facing local families, schools and public services. Professionally, he has worked at the BBC, the Department for Culture Media and Sport, Ofcom, in academia, and now at Deloitte, where he challenges tech companies to make safer products.

As a Green councillor, Brijesh will leverage his experience to strengthen services, support schools and make sure council decisions are informed and rooted in residents’ needs.

Asli has lived in Bounds Green for the past 18 years, and cares deeply about the local area and the wonderful community we have here.

As a seasoned, professional interpreter, Asli has developed a profound understanding of the local issues and the specific needs of her neighbours. Her extensive experience enables her to connect with community members on a personal level, fostering trust and open communication.

In addition to her interpreting work, she is deeply committed to advocating for families facing housing challenges, recognising the vital link between stable housing, health, and education.

Asli is passionate about empowering those around her, ensuring that everyone has access to the resources and support they need to thrive.

**Our Priorities for Bounds Green**

**Competence**
We both live right here in Bounds Green, and are running to be your local councillors because we care about fixing the basics.

From dealing with dangerous junctions to fixing lighting and pavements, we will stand up for Bounds Green.

We need a council, and local councillors, that help, listen, and respond to residents' concerns.

**Compassion**
We will keep residents safe by cracking down on rogue landlords, fixing dangerous defects and ending damp and mould in social housing and pushing to restore the size of the police team in Bounds Green.

**Community**
After leading the creation of our community pub, we will take that energy to reinvigorate our parks and public spaces, listening to residents, and putting their voices at the heart of our actions.

I feel inseparable to our local community, which has been home to me and my family for over 10 years. Over the years, I have driven local Labour Party initiatives to improve social housing, enhance green spaces, and make our streets safer for everyone. I am a proud Unison member and will serve our community with care and enthusiasm. I’m also passionate about renewable energy and dedicated to promoting a sustainable environment. I would treasure the opportunity to work with you all to make Bruce Castle an even more wonderful place to live, work, and visit.

Dr Imad Ahmed
Imad has been a dedicated community campaigner for over 20 years. With a professional background in negotiating complex government investments and projects, he has the expertise to ensure Haringey Council truly delivers for residents.

Driven by a commitment to improving people’s lives, his experience ranges from international human rights through to local improvements, such as the greening of his own street with 14 new trees.

Imad will ensure our leisure centres and town centre are prioritised, protect our parks and libraries, and champion vocational training for all.

All posts promoted by K Riddle on behalf Imad Ahmed (Haringey Liberal Democrats) at Ashley House, 235-239 High Road, N22 8HF.

Rimil Hembrom has lived in Haringey for more than four years and has been inspired by the borough’s strong tradition of solidarity and resistance to division. A first-generation migrant, active trade unionist and long-time campaigner, he wants to build on that culture of care in local government. Rimil is standing because he believes the council should be more open, more democratic and more closely connected to the communities it serves. He wants to create more ways for residents to shape local decisions and will support stronger grassroots networks across the borough. His priorities include participation, mutual support, dignity, and protecting the local environment for future generations.

Politics for Rimil are about looking out for the most vulnerable people and ensuring that the local infrastructure and services work in their favour, providing support they need. If elected, he will be a hard-working local councillor who is there for local residents to support them with their issues.

I’m proud to stand as a Labour candidate in Fortis Green, having lived locally for 25 years. As a project manager professionally and a community activist, our standard of living and how we care for and educate our children are my priorities. I will work hard to ensure residents are really listened to. Haringey’s Labour Council is already making real change, supporting residents and families across the borough. All of our Children’s Services rated ‘Outstanding’ and 98% of schools are ‘Good’ or ‘Outstanding. But there’s always more to do.

Fortis Green deserves a visible voice that fights for you. Together, we’ll build a greener and safer Fortis Green.

I am an architect, specialising in conservation, who has lived and worked here locally for 20 years.
I want to improve housing quality, public spaces and climate resilience by bringing long-term , resident focused thinking to local decisions - making Muswell Hill an even more special place.
As a Liberal Democrat, giving back to the community, amplifying residents’ voices and supporting those most in need are, of course, central to why I want to be your local champion: to follow in the long tradition of Lib Dem councillors in Muswell Hill, started nearly three decades ago by Lynne Featherstone and continued most recently with Councillor Pippa Connor.

Florence Wright is a lifelong resident of Muswell Hill. Alongside completing her Master’s degree at the LSE in Societal and Environmental psychology, she works in Green MP Ellie Chowns’s Parliamentary office. Florence has been involved in climate justice campaigning for years, and locally coordinates the Eco Group at St James Church. She is also a long-term volunteer at Wave Hub Muswell Hill, an inclusive cafe for people with and without learning disabilities to socialise and work together. Her priorities include clean air, protecting green spaces, supporting local businesses, strengthening youth services, and making Muswell Hill more inclusive and accessible for everyone.

Noel Park has been my home for over 30 years; its vibrant energy, greenery, and diverse cultures make it one of a kind. I know the ward deeply, and I want to stand up and advocate for all who rely on our services. Through my role at Healthwatch Haringey, I look at how services function and how residents’ experiences shape them. My approach to the role of Councillor would be to keep residents at the centre, challenging where things fall short, and working to drive improvements that reflect people’s needs. Fairness, community, and respect are the values that drive me.

My Councillor focus has been listening to residents and improving Northumberland Park.  As Cabinet Member for Communities, I’ve co-designed new plans for the station including better Spurs event co-ordination. I’ve been involved in the Clear, Hold, Build initiative to reduce crime and boost safety. Labour’s values of equality, fairness, and social justice are my values. Working with local residents we’ve achieved closure orders on problem estates, delivered Willoughby Lane’s zebra crossing and added ‘unsuitable for HGVs’ signage. We’ve enhanced our green areas, Somerford Grove and Hartington Park. I’m determined to keep driving progress.

I'm a local resident with children at school in St Ann's. I'm standing because I believe in the importance of committed, practical and effective local representation rooted in Labour values

I want to ensure that residents' concerns are heard and addressed. I already have a strong track record of working with the Council to protect and improve St Ann's.

I believe that the only way of achieving change is to have a voice at the table when decisions are being made. I will be that strong voice for you in St Ann's.

I am a doctor and GP trainee, born and raised here, shaped by my heritage and strengthened by my faith. Tottenham is where I learned to fight for my community, for dignity, and for justice, and I have never stopped. I work at the intersection of women’s health, public health and racial inequality. I understand how poor housing, unsafe streets and limited access to services shape people’s health long before they reach a clinic. I have supported international medical aid, education and emergency relief from the UK to Gambia and Gaza.

Haringey has been my home for more than 30 years. I’ve seen first-hand the strong sense of community that brings people together here. Through my work supporting autistic children and young people, I see every day how important patience, understanding and the right support can be in helping people thrive. Those experiences shape the way I approach community engagement, listening to residents, supporting local services and helping keep West Green a welcoming and thriving place for everyone who lives here. Fairness, community and respect are the values that guide me.

Communities are carrying the burden of a system that is failing them. Years of deep public service cuts and now the cost-of-living crisis have made life even harder, while local rents continue to increase unchecked. Our community deserves better! Elect us to stand against inequality and racism and for decent services.

We are an alliance of socialists from local grassroots community groups, campaigning groups in the Borough and local community activists. We are committed to transforming local politics in Haringey. We aspire to build an inclusive and democratic alliance to provide a political alternative to the establishment in Haringey and to challenge the rotten Labour Party leadership of the Council that is imposing Austerity on residents. We want to join with and amplify the voices of social justice campaigns across Haringey. We oppose all cuts in services, the wealthy should pay for decent Public Services including housing, health, education. We oppose the privatisation of public services for profit!

On Housing we will:
Expand social home building and provide appropriate accommodation for all our communities
Call off evictions and the bailiffs and enforce legislation against unlawful and unfair evictions
Campaign for rent controls
Make private landlords implement repairs and refurbishment to provide clean quality rented accommodation for all our residents
Make a permanent plan to repair and improve all social housing
Change Haringey’s planning priorities to serve Haringey residents not property developers and speculators
Stop selling off Haringey Council property and assets

On Poverty we will:
Change the narrative! Those in poverty will not be blamed, we will blame those who are making themselves rich off the backs off the poor and the politicians who support the rich
Fight for justice for all benefits claimants and oppose all benefit cuts
Build local community hubs to build solidarity, agency and advocacy for our residents
Campaign for free public transport in London

On the Climate Catastrophe we will
Take all the necessary measures to provide clean air and water for Haringey
Implement policies that require all building and transport infrastructure to meet the standards of the green agenda
Stop investing in the oil multinationals
Continue to oppose and block the building of Incinerators for power generation

On Community Organising we will:
Support the right to food, implement policies that stop children going hungry and work with community organisations to ensure all have good nutrition and dignity in accessing food
Support all community food growing projects and initiatives
Refurbish and expand community spaces and centres ensuring all local groups have the community spaces they want
Develop play, sport and recreation spaces for all our children and adults

On unity against racism & discrimination we will:
Strengthen language support and boost integration of communities
Decolonize local education, ban exclusions and expand all education for migrants to prosper in Haringey because migrants are welcome in Haringey
Not co-operate with Prevent or Immigration raids or police targeting minorities
Defend Trans rights, women's rights and disability rights

On Israel's Genocide we will:
End all investment in businesses that are trading with or in Israel
Develop and implement and policy of ethical investment that stops investment in businesses associated with military, war and genocide
Stop banking with Barclays
Support Workers' direct action boycott of Israel, war, genocide and campaign for all trade-unions in Haringey to support the boycott of genocide across the world
Support the self-determination of the Palestinian people and the Kurdish people

We stand firmly on the principle of building a just and inclusive society—one that is anti-racist, anti-fascist, anti-austerity, anti-war and anti-imperialist. We are committed to defending and expanding high-quality, publicly owned services which are developed in meaningful partnership with the residents who need them.

Tough times demand experienced leadership and that’s why I’m standing again, to use every bit of my local knowledge to deliver more funding for our green spaces, safer roads and streets for our families. My pragmatic leadership delivers for our community. I was born here. I grew up here and since 2022, I’ve served as White Hart Lane Councillor, as a recent Mayor and former Chair of the Pensions Board. The economic challenges ahead are real but we’ve achieved change together. Let’s finish what we started, we can build a White Hart Lane that keeps excelling for our community.

White Hart Lane’s diverse and welcoming communities deserve safety and economic growth. I co-founded a local Community Centre in Tottenham. I’ve seen how strong Labour-led Haringey Council services improve lives. I want to build on that, with even more investment in social housing, green spaces, and fair economic chances for all. Haringey’s diversity is its greatest strength - I want to nurture it. I’m running to ensure the council reflects every community it serves. I’m dedicated to building trust and working with residents. Together, we can keep White Hart Lane a place where all feel heard and represented.

Shaan Rafiq is proud to come from a tradition of community care – his mother was a dinner lady who became a foster carer, his father a cab driver who became a teacher. Today, Shaan is a software engineer and a foster carer himself. Living in Haringey for the past 3 years, Shaan is standing because he wants local people to have a councillor who understands how to help residents navigate systems that can often feel confusing or unwelcoming. He is particularly interested in supporting those who need help to speak up for themselves, and in making sure council decisions are guided by fairness rather than profit.

I grew up in Haringey and it shaped who I am. Coming from a Kurdish Alevi background, I learned early what it feels like to be unheard. I’m determined that every resident has someone who will stand up for them. Through working at the Alevi Centre, I’ve supported people with housing, family and personal challenges. I’m someone others can rely on. As I’ve lived here most of my life, I understand the issues people deal with every day. If elected, I’ll be a visible councillor who listens and takes action. I want to serve, and give back to our community.

As your Woodside councilor, I’ve delivered on your priorities. Together with Haringey’s Labour Council, we’ve delivered a new children’s playground in Woodside Park, with another £350k being invested in WHL recreation. Along Perth Road and nearby streets, we’ve worked with residents to co-design traffic calming measures and make our roads safer. And on ASB, we’ve teamed up with local people, council officers, and the police to tackle problems on Lordship Lane and make our streets safer for our community. There is much more to do, and I want to be that person representing your voice.

Dear residents and businesses of Harrow Weald,

Harrow Weald is more than just a place — it’s our shared home, built on hard work, community spirit, and local pride. But I know many of you feel that everyday challenges are being overlooked.

Residents want safer streets, cleaner parks, reliable healthcare, and housing that respects the character of our area. Families want better opportunities for young people, less congestion on our roads, and neighbourhoods they can feel proud of.

Our local businesses are the backbone of Harrow Weald. They need better support — fair access, safer high streets, reduced anti-social behaviour, and a thriving local economy where they can grow and succeed.

I am standing to listen and to act.

My commitment is simple:
• Safer, cleaner streets for everyone
• Stronger support for local businesses
• Better access to healthcare and community services
• Sensible planning that protects our area while allowing it to grow responsibly

Together, we can build a Harrow Weald that works for everyone — residents, families, and businesses alike.

Vote for a stronger, local voice. Vote for change.

Ahsan Bin Talib
A Real Voice for Harrow Weald

A Harrow resident of 27 years, Farid has spent 20 years supporting vulnerable people through local and regional charities. Today, he leads a charity helping local children with maths, English, coding and wellbeing.

He will bring this lifelong commitment to practical, compassionate action to the council, fighting for every resident.

Yihya works in education. Living in Harrow for over 20 years, he has long-standing community ties and involvement in community initiatives, including on food poverty, luxury housing developments that ignore the needs of local people, and supporting vulnerable residents.

He is a strong supporter of local businesses, promoting their role in building a thriving and resilient community.

Born and raised in Harrow, Faran's work has included helping people into emergency housing, and he regularly volunteers at a Wealdstone-based charity assisting families in poverty.

Faran is dedicated to supporting local businesses and wants to work on revitalising Wealdstone High Street.

Wealdstone native Dante has experience supporting residents by working at food banks and advocating for private renters against rogue landlords.

He has campaigned in Harrow against racism and food poverty over recent years and is secretary of a local organisation for Palestinian rights.

Tito is the Director of Nourishment, a community and creative agency that bridges the gap between creatives and young professionals with cultural institutions through events and content. Tito takes a particular interest in how technology & innovation policy can be leveraged to tackle social challenges, particularly in diverse and low-income communities, and has pursued this through research in academia.

Ghazala moved to UK as a child almost 50 years ago. Having lived in Havering for over 35 years and brought up a family of 4 kids and as a working Mum she knows what pressures ordinary folks face on daily basis. With her kids all now grown up, she is keen to be actively engaged in the local community. She has been a parent school governor and she has supported her husband's work in the NHS. Ghazala left school at 16 to care for her mother and younger siblings and went back into education later in life. She is passionate about everyone deserving a second chance and is particularly keen for the local council to support young people in our community, to unlock their potential. Has had various careers and is currently a dedicated driving instructor.

I've proudly called Harold Hill home for over 22 years. From the moment I moved here from North London, I felt the strong sense of community and care that makes this area so special to me. It's where my children were born and raised, and where my family runs our local business. I've always felt supported here, and truly value the friendly atmosphere, our beautiful parks, and the deer that make Harold Hill unique.

I am a Civil Engineer working in highways, specialising in infrastructure and development. With over 14 years of experience, I understand how decisions are made, how funding is used, and how we can deliver real, practical improvements to our roads, services, and neighbourhoods. Alongside my professional work, I have always been actively involved in supporting people in the community-helping with forms, job applications, and accessing services, particularly for elderly and low- income residents. Being bilingual has allowed me to support people who might otherwise struggle to be heard.

My personal experiences have shaped who I am. Supporting a brother with learning difficulties and another with mental health challenges has given me resilience, empathy, and a strong sense of responsibility. As a parent of twins, balancing home life, career, and a family business.

I care strongly about protecting and supporting families and strengthening our community. I believe that when people are given fair opportunities and the right support, they can build better lives-not just for themselves, but for others around them.

Anish grew up in Aldborough Hatch and has lived in Harold Wood for 5 years. Graduating from King's College London in 2010, he is a dentist in east London and Essex, and worked for many years as an NHS dentist in Harold Hill.

He believes that the decimation of local services, as well as local disruption and environmental destruction caused by huge private interests, and the breakdown of affordable healthcare and housing, are all symptomatic of wider economic collapse.

The Green Party is the only party that supports taxing wealth, not work.

Anish believes that without stopping the rapid redistribution of wealth up to the already extremely wealthy, not only will climate change continue to accelerate and the cost of living continue to rise, but generations to come will never afford decent housing, basic healthcare or dignity in old age.
Manon has experience in change management and process improvement. She has lived in Romford for 9 years and wants Havering to be a place where everyone is welcome, supported and proud of their area.

About Mushtaq Ahmad.
Profession: Outreach Manager  
Education: BA (Hons.) Economics & Development Studies (SOAS: London), Former President of SOAS Students' Union
Resident of South Hornchurch Ward.

Candidate will strive for...

1. Investment in roads, lamposts for the improvements of the ward’s infrastructure. Many local roads have potholes and in need of new ashphalting.  Reflecting the fact, there has been no investment in local infrastructure for the last few years.

2. Currently there is no community space in the ward.  One centre that exists is not serving the communitys' needs.  It is vitally important to have sufficient community space to serve the growing community.

3. Library is not just a learning space but where people come together from all walks of life for learning and social cohesion. We need our library back.

4. For a community to flourish and to create a culture of care for everyone and togetherness. I want to bring the residents, especially its’ senior citizens, who contributed much to the ward under one roof to revitalise community spirit.

5. Food Bank – I want to forge links with the local supermarkets to supply surplus of food items to be distributed for free for people in need in the Ward.

6. This ward has gone through enormous demographic changes in the last few years, many people have made their home here.  Our representation body needs to reflect this demographic change and embrace it for the good.

7. There have been reports of crime such as burglaries; this must stop by making law enforcement more effective.

8. Unnecessary restriction on car movements in school areas creating much inconvenience for many residents, including incurrence of fines.  We must review this to make and keep transportation normal for everyone.  Children safety around school areas needs to be reviewed in light to alternative effective measures such as speed limit and cameras in place to enforce it rather than complete ban on cars during certain hours.

9. In built up areas such as on South End Road there must be speed limits enforced including cameras, speed bumps to reduce accidents and improvement the safety of residents.

10. Allocation of council housing must be prioritised for those who live in the ward.

Vote your Independent candidate Mushtaq Ahmad for a fresh approach and change for a better ward and borough.

Madhu works for our local hospital and sees the pressure our health services are under. He wants to improve health outcomes for all ages, a proactive approach to tackling drug harms and champion better infrastructure planning.

A little bit about me: I live in Hillingdon, West London, and have proudly served as an NHS GP in a practice in the neighbouring constituency of Hayes and Harlington for more than 20 years. My commitment to public service extends beyond healthcare, as I also serve as a School Governor for a local school in Hayes and as a Charity Trustee for a local organisation, that supports older people and those in need across Hillingdon.

Born in Barnet, North London, I attended a primary school in Colindale before my family returned to India. It was there that I spent my childhood and youth and pursued my dreams of becoming a doctor. I have been a resident of Hillingdon since 2016,  living with my husband, in-laws, and my children.

So why am I asking for your support? 

Throughout my entire career, I have been deeply involved in public service. As a GP, I have not only listened to my patients but also acted as their advocate. I have worked in various hospitals around the country including West Middlesex hospital where I worked as a junior doctor in the maternity unit. I have been instrumental in policy formulation and service redesign as a Clinical Leader in North West London, and worked closely with GP colleagues in Hounslow. Additionally, my role as a Board Member of a large Mental Health and Community NHS Trust, as well as my position as a Lead for Nursing Homes in my practice area, have provided me with diverse perspectives on healthcare and social care issues.

My experience as a School Governor has given me valuable insight into our education system and the challenges it faces. These experiences, together with my ability to empathise, listen, and understand people's needs, have led me to believe that politics is the arena where I can make a tangible difference, which is why I started my journey over a year ago. I am eager to utilise my personal and professional skills, accumulated knowledge, and understanding of people's concerns to serve you and the wider society.

I invite you to join me on this journey. Let us work together to create positive change and make our voices heard. Stay tuned for campaign updates, events, and opportunities to connect. Your support and engagement are crucial and deeply appreciated.

Thank you for taking the time to learn more about me and my campaign. I am eager to meet you, listen to your ideas, and pursue the matters that are important to you. Together, let's shape a brighter future.

Being a lifelong Hillingdon resident, I have seen the excellent work the Conservative Council have done for many years, and I wish to build on that.

I am proud to see how tidy and well kept my local area is, and I know that is due to the hard work of Conservative Councillors who ensure our bins are collected weekly, and our services are well run. I equally see the difference of Labour run Ealing and Hounslow Council who charge their residents a lot more money, and for a poorer quality of services.

I hope I can rely on your support to ensure that we retain Conservative Councillors and that our much loved services are protected and maintained to the standard that we deserve.

Svavar is proud to call Ruislip Gardens and South Ruislip home. Having lived in the area since 2001, Svavar felt a sense of belonging from the moment he arrived. Surrounded by natural beauty and welcoming neighbours, Svavar values the green spaces and the kindness he encounters throughout the community.

In his professional life, Svavar works in IT for a large multinational corporation. He is experienced in building consensus, bringing people together around shared goals, and leading with empathy. Svavar enjoys hearing a range of perspectives and is unafraid to be persistent when it matters.

Svavar considers himself fortunate to live comfortably, and he wants the same security and quality of life for everyone in the community. He believes that life improves for all when people support one another, and that local government has a vital role to play in making everyday life better. In particular, Svavar is committed to ensuring that social and elder care is delivered with compassion, that vulnerable residents receive the support they need, and that children have the best possible start in life. Alongside this, Svavar champions excellent outdoor spaces, strong local businesses, and a thriving community. He believes there is real hope for positive change, and that working together is how it happens.

Anil is local to the area and has worked as a cardiologist and acute physician for nearly 30 years, with a lot of that time at Hillingdon Hospital. He is now semi-retired and focusing on work in the community.

Vote for Anil for a considerate Conservative councillor who works hard for residents and puts them first.

I live in Hayes and work in local
government, where I focus on
improving services and
supporting residents. I
understand how council
decisions affect daily life, and I
use my experience to speak up
for fair and practical action.
I am already involved in raising
concerns about safety, housing
conditions, cleaner streets and
better local services. I listen to
residents, follow up on issues
and work to get real solutions.
For Wood End Ward, I want to
help create safer
neighbourhoods, improve the
local environment, support
families and young people and
make sure the council uses
money wisely. My aim is to be a
strong voice for the community
and to put residents first.

I have lived in Hounslow for over 30 years, where I raised my family and built strong local roots through decades of volunteering and community service.

After completing my MBA at the University of Liverpool, I have successfully garnered a senior leadership career in multinational IT companies.

I bring proven organisational and problem-solving skills, alongside a long-standing commitment to health, wellbeing and the environment, support for cancer fundraising, support for vunerable women, as well as hands-on work to improve local neighbourhoods and green spaces.

My council related articles can be seen here https://chiswick-labour.co.uk/blogs.php

Please visit www.chiswick-labour.co.uk to learn more about how I and the other members of the Labour team will keep Chiswick beautiful.

My name is Hira Dhillon, and I am standing to be the next local councillor for Cranford Ward following the sad passing of Cllr Sukhbir Dhaliwal.

Cranford is my home and it has been for 58 years. I have been involved in our community for a long time. I was a parent govenor of Cranford Junior School. I am currently a member of the Cranford Ward Panel, linking residents with the Police and Hounslow Council, and the Cranford Action Group which helps keep our streets clean.

I am retired now, but I spent most of my life working on the London Underground and railways as a guard, driver, and booking clerk.

It would be the honour of a lifetime to be chosen by my fellow residents of Cranford to represent them on Hounslow Council.

My pledges to you:

1. A local voice
I've lived here for nearly 60 years. I love Cranford and I will always put our community first.

2. Zero tolerance on antisocial behaviour
I'm already a member of the local Group which links residents with the Police and Hounslow Council. As your local Councillor I'll keep pushing police and the council to make Cranford safer.

3. State of the art Community Hub
Labour are building a new community centre and library at Meadowbank. I'll also be working with the Council to make sure that the current library site is used in a way which best suits Cranford.

4. Tough on flytipping and littering
Hounslow Labour have raised fines to the maximum legal limit, now we need to see them handed out to those who disrespect Cranford's streets and green spaces.

Renu has lived in Cranford for over 14 years - it is her home and her community. She understands the everyday issues residents face, from traffic and fly-tipping to GP access and the need for safer, cleaner streets. These are not abstract concerns, but part of daily life. She brings over 20 years of experience as a legal expert, mediator, and business leader, with a strong focus on resolving conflict, supporting communities, and standing up for fairness. Renu is standing in Cranford because the community deserves committed, visible, and effective representation.

I've been a resident of Hounslow for over a decade and observed the frustrating lack of care our wards are receiving.   As a councillor I would fight for more environmentally friendly policies, cleaner and safer streets, as well as more involvement of local community in the affairs of the ward.

Riza was born in Istanbul, Türkiye, in 1965. He went on to graduate with a degree in Electronics and Telecommunications Engineering, beginning his career with Turkish Telecom PLC. After moving to the UK in 1994, Riza continued to build on his expertise by gaining further qualifications in Electrical and Electronic Engineering, including ONC and HNC certifications. Over the years, he has worked with a range of leading lighting control companies, including Philips, Cooper, Eaton, Prolojik, and Leax, developing extensive experience within the industry. He currently works as an R&D Manager at TheisCraft Ltd, a company specialising in energy-saving and intelligent lighting control systems. Riza has lived in Feltham for over 30 years with his partner, Frances, and remains closely connected to the local community.

Josefina has lived in Hounslow for 17 years, who is currently self-employed.
She studied politics and international relations.

Mohan Bains is an experienced business leader and international advisor. He is a former President of the Asian Business Forum at the Thames Valley Chamber of Commerce in the UK and has served as an Adviser on European foreign investment to the Government of India, focusing on Haryana. He brings strong expertise in international trade, investment, and economic development.

Suresh has lived in Hounslow for 25 years with his wife and children. Over the years, he has developed a deep understanding of the community and the issues that matter most to local residents — from street safety and youth unemployment to the daily challenges faced by the elderly. He is standing for Councillor to be a true champion for the community, committed to listening, supporting, and delivering real change for everyone in Hounslow.

Ragveer Mattu is a dedicated community advocate and charity leader with more than 25 years of experience supporting disabled adults, vulnerable residents and low‑income families across London. Born in Hillingdon and raised in Ealing, Ragveer has lived in the
London Borough of Hounslow for over three decades, building deep local roots and a strong understanding of the community they serve.
Ragveer is currently studying at university to strengthen their understanding of legal, social
and policy issues. This ongoing learning reflects a long‑standing commitment to fairness, justice and ensuring that residents who are often overlooked receive the support they deserve. Ragveer’s political involvement began in 2010 when they stood as a Conservative candidate in the local elections. Since then, Ragveer has remained active in local politics,
working closely with councillors, party members and community organisations. They also use social media as a tool to highlight disability rights, raise awareness of local challenges and stay connected with residents across the borough. In the voluntary sector, Ragveer serves as Chairman of a charity supporting children with additional needs and their families. Through this work, they help provide essential guidance, practical support and reliable information to families navigating complex challenges. Ragveer also chairs a community food bank that distributes surplus supermarket food to disabled adults, elderly residents, low‑income households and
homeless individuals across London and Surrey. Operating seven days a week through a
dedicated team of volunteers, the service has become a vital lifeline for many.
Over the years, Ragveer has assisted thousands of residents facing difficulties related to housing, benefits, homelessness, council tax and financial hardship. Their hands‑on experience, compassion and commitment to public service continue to guide their mission: ensuring every resident is listened to, respected and supported.

Syed has lived and worked in Hounslow for over 30 years and understands the daily challenges local families face. Standing for the Liberal Democrats, he is committed to tackling anti-social behaviour, improving street cleanliness, and driving ambitious town regeneration. He aims to be a councillor who truly listens to ensure the community remains a place residents can be proud of.

Miruna has lived in Hounslow South for five years and works in public affairs, while also helping out at a local pub. She is an active community volunteer, collecting donations for charities supporting animals and children. Miruna is passionate about helping residents, including members of the Eastern European community, understand their rights and get the support they need.

Krishna has lived in Hounslow for 25 years and has been a resident of Hounslow South for the past 15 years, where he lives with his wife and children. He actively works with residents to address local issues such as illegal parking, fly-tipping, and road and pavement repairs, and has helped organise community street play events for children. Krishna understands the priorities of the local community and, if elected, will work hard to improve the area and ensure stronger accountability in local governance.

As a Hounslow resident of over a decade, I am an Educator, UCL Education Specialist, and Property Expert with a decade of experience in the housing sector. I combine this practical expertise with the analytical rigour of my MSc in Global Business Management (University of Roehampton) and BSc in Physics to advocate for higher standards in Isleworth. My approach to local governance is rooted in transparent, SMART [(S)pecific, (M)easurable, (A)chievable, (R)elevant, (T)ime-bound] accountability. By championing STEM skills and data-driven public services, I work to ensure our residents' needs remain the focus of every decision.

Sapphire was born in Camden, London, raised in the Borough of Brent, and has lived in Hounslow for nearly 40 years, with deep and long‑standing roots in the borough. Their children were born and raised locally, giving Sapphire a strong personal connection to the community and a clear understanding of the realities faced by local families.

Sapphire is the founder and trustee of a registered charity supporting disabled children and their families. Informed by lived experience—with Sapphire’s own child being the inspiration behind the charity’s formation—this work is grounded in a first‑hand understanding of the challenges families face when navigating services, accessing support, and understanding rights and entitlements. The charity’s work focuses on reducing barriers, improving access, and ensuring families are supported in a fair, informed, and respectful way.

In addition, Sapphire is the founder and trustee of a foodbank supporting elderly people, individuals experiencing homelessness, people with disabilities, and low‑income families. Through this role, Sapphire has helped deliver practical support to some of the borough’s most vulnerable residents, while ensuring services operate within appropriate safeguarding, regulatory, and governance frameworks. This work has strengthened local partnerships and contributed to more consistent and accountable community support.

Alongside charitable responsibilities, Sapphire is currently studying at university, developing advanced knowledge of legal frameworks, policy interpretation, and structured decision‑making. This knowledge supports a more informed, rights‑based approach to community work, helping local people better understand processes, challenge barriers appropriately, and access services and support to which they are entitled.

Together, this combination of long‑term local knowledge, lived experience, and legally informed community leadership contributes to positive outcomes for residents. It supports stronger advocacy, clearer engagement with services, and more effective community support, helping ensure that individuals and families in Hounslow are treated fairly, consistently, and with dignity—now and in the future.

I have lived in Godmanchester for the past 15 years. I am a former Town Councillor and a active local campaigner and a community champion. I will fight for your rights, I have a burning desire to be a councillor and love to serve Community. I hope you will encourage me and elect me as a counsellor

statement_to_voters:
My name is Rasan. I grew up in North London and now live in Laycock, working across local GP practices. I’m Kurdish, and my family came to Britain when I was a baby after fleeing conflict in the Middle East. That has shaped how I think about community, security, and the importance of feeling supported where you live. In my work, I spend a lot of time talking to people about their health, and you quickly see how often the same issues come up: housing, stress, money, isolation, and the environment around us. It’s a big part of why I care about local politics. Health isn’t just what happens in a clinic. It’s shaped by the conditions we live in.

I’m standing because I want an Islington where people can live well, with strong local services, decent housing, and communities that feel supported. I’ll back practical local action that improves health, fairness, and quality of life.

Devon was just 124 votes away from becoming the first Green Councillor in Tufnell Park in 2022. She’s worked and volunteered in community centres and programmes across Islington over the last 7 years, and her priority would be to make sure that residents can easily access the support they need.

“Tufnell Park is my home. I want to work with you to make sure that the day-to-day things that would make your life easier are being done by the council”, Devon said. “Actions speak louder than words — that’s why I’ve reported over 100 potholes this year. When I tell you I want Tufnell Park to be a safer, better place to live, you can see we’re already doing the hard work”.

Much of her local paid and voluntary work has enabled her to build deep relationships with residents over many years, listening with patience and respect to the range of diverse viewpoints that make up our community. She would continue to fight for all of your everyday problems like bins and drains to quality of life issues like damp and mould.

statement_to_voters:
I was raised in Kensington, and this community has shaped who I am. I’ve seen first hand the challenges families face, and I’ve spent years working locally to support residents and strengthen our neighbourhood. I chaired our local residents’ association for two and a half years, led our Area Review Board, worked in RBKC’s voluntary sector, and carried out casework for our former MP, Emma Dent Coad. These experiences have grounded my commitment to listening, acting, and delivering for our community.

I founded Homemade Teachers, an organisation that supports parents in helping their children succeed and better understand the primary curriculum. This is because when families are empowered, our whole community thrives. As a teacher, I’ve taken GCSE students abroad to broaden their horizons and explore different cultures. I also volunteered internationally during my gap year in North India and organised annual excursions to Spain, experiences that deepened my commitment to equality, opportunity, and global responsibility.

I am a graduate of Queen Mary University of London, with a postgraduate degree from the University of Cambridge, specialising in Geography and Education. I bring a strong commitment to social and environmental justice.

It would be a privilege to represent the residents of Dalgarno Ward. I am determined to ensure that the voices of those living in the north of the borough are heard, and that every concern is met with fairness and persistence.

At the moment we are being let down.
 The council’s recent low grading from the social housing regulator highlights serious failures to meet Decent Homes standards. This is unacceptable, especially in a ward where more than 65% of residents live in social housing. Everyone deserves a safe, warm, and healthy home. Without it, our health, our children’s education, and our opportunities all suffer.

Together, we can achieve.
 Let’s build a greener, fairer, and more compassionate future for Dalgarno.

I’ve worked with schools, community partners and youth services to tackle antisocial behaviour, keep young people in schools and demand support for children with SEND. I’m committed to defending our most vulnerable residents and fighting for a safer, fairer and more resilient community.

statement_to_voters: Do you want a progressive local councillor who will stand up for the issues you care about, from fly-tipping to potholes to investing in our local assets, then I am your candidate.

I’m Justyna, your Green Party candidate for Beverley and Newland. I have lived all of my adult life in Hull, and most of it in this exact ward.The people of Hull have been neglected by the current Lib Dem administration, and I believe it’s up to people like myself and my fellow Green Party candidates to stand up and demand better. The Lib Dems have wasted their time in charge of the council and are scaremongering about Reform to save their seat. Labour are facing wipeout.

We at the Green Party are here to take over.I want Beverley and Newland to thrive as the multi-cultural community that it is.

Local resident Shaune Clarkson to fight Marfleet ward for Hull Labour in May's election.

✊ A recently retired trade union officer for the GMB, Shaune previously worked at Crown Paints in Hull for many years, where he stuck up for his work mates as their union rep - now he's ready to stick up for residents in Marfleet ward as their councillor.

🏠 Shaune has lived in our local area for more than 20 years, on Amethyst Road, with his wife and son.

Shaune takes over from Cllr Patrick Wilkinson, who's stepping down from the Council after four years of serving Marfleet residents. ❤️

Hi, I’m Amazon.
I was born and raised in Hull and work for the NHS. I care deeply about our city and its communities having previously been awarded a Civic Crown award for my services working to raise awareness of certain marginalised communities. I’m a keen gardener and I also occasionally find the time to perform stand-up comedy, which I see more as a hobby than I do a career aspiration.

I always speak up when something isn’t right and will always put myself on the line when vital action is needed. It would be an honour to represent the people of Orchard Park on behalf of the Green Party and I’m excited to do all that I can in the short time we have left before the local elections.

party_description_text: Kingston Independent Residents - Green Community Voice
organisation_name: Royal Borough of Kingston upon Thames
Royal Borough of Kingston upon Thames
statement_to_voters:
Having lived around Kingston all my life, I've always loved the strong encouragement of arts, our beautiful green spaces, and the diversity of shops and cuisines. Since moving in to the borough itself, I find myself falling in love with the communities and opportunities around, and I endeavour to continue bringing out and developing the best Kingston Town has to offer.

Coming from a low-income background, and being care experienced, I find myself connecting with residents all around Kingston about the struggles faced as a result of poor services, high council tax, and worries about the future if these conditions continue. As a renter, I understand that soaring housing prices only add to those fears. Kingston Town needs more affordable and social housing, and services which are worth their costs. Following the examples of local Labour boroughs like Merton, I aim to push the council towards council-run services, and a local plan with social and affordable housing as a priority. We can see all over Kingston that when profit comes before people, we get unaffordable high rises that drive local residents away instead of housing them. Alongside my colleagues, I aim to put residents and their needs at the forefront, rather than modern looking housing which serves no one.

As the eldest of four, I see firsthand the impact of declining spaces for children and young people. I've been there, trying to find places for my siblings to attend youth clubs or teen-friendly events, and I share the same concerns as so many parents and grandparents; we aren't providing enough for our children, and this must change. As someone who was never a fan of sports, due my disability, I worry for the young people like me, who don't want to or cannot participate in sports clubs, whose only options are to find parks to hang around at with friends. In a borough with such love of street art, theatre, and diverse festivals and musical events, we don't provide nearly enough of these opportunities to our children. I aim to work with Achieving for Children, local schools, and our wealth of artistic organisations, to bring more opportunities to our youth. I also intend to bring more awareness of council grants and funding, helping to fund free spaces for disadvantaged youths, making sure we provide for our children.

As Secretary of the Kingston Disability Network, it is my duty to understand and advocate for the needs of disabled residents and organisations across the borough. I know there is still so much more to be done to support disabled residents, but I believe that a combination of lived experiences like my own and an open mind in listening to residents is key to working towards equal access and opportunity in Kingston. I also recognise how deeply accessibility benefits communities overall, with its positive impacts touching more than just those with disabilities. Improving street signage and markings helps all of us to move safely around. Running more bus routes means not only do mobility impaired residents have a greater chance of getting a seat and reduced likelihood of injury, but parents can board with their buggies without worrying their children will be bashed or tripped over, encouraging them to take trips out.

On the doors in Kingston, we hear so often about bad rubbish collections leaving waste across the streets, and even with amazing local groups like the Friends of Fairfield Park, our green spaces could still use some extra love, especially the areas without a Friends of group to keep them clean and litter-free. In Council, I aim to push connections between Glendale and local communities, making it quick and easy for residents to arrange local clean ups, and ensuring our contractors do their fair share. I lead by example, having organised and attended park clean ups, and in connection with local organisations like the Kingston Environment Centre, I know our borough is so well equipped to shine, if only we make it easier to take those steps.

Our local businesses make Kingston Town such a wonderful place to visit. Still, we hear all too often of small businesses struggling to keep afloat with high business costs and rents. I aim to foster closer connections with Kingston First to support our small businesses, as well as taking time to share our local gems. It's so important to support these often family run shops, and I strive to work closely with owners to identify and solve the problems they face.

statement_to_voters: Raju has lived in Surbiton since 1968. He studied at Hollyfield Secondary School and worked for BT until he took early release in March 2012. His community voluntary work over the past thirty-five years includes as Local Authority school governor, and as an elected Councillor in Kingston from 1994 to 2002. He was a founder member of the Kingston Group for Religious Understanding which has now become Kingston Racial Equality Council.

statement_to_voters: Jamila has been running her own business for the last 12 years in Kingston. In that time she has got to know the residents and issues concerning Kingston town centre. She is passionate about making Kingston a welcoming place for all by protecting green spaces, investment in parks and stopping anti-social behaviour. Jamila volunteers for a wide range of charities and community groups, working families and children to give them self confidence, including girls guiding, supporting a soup kitchen and a domestic violence charity.
