statement_to_voters:
I have lived in London for 10 years, the day I became a citizen, I knew I wanted to give something back to the community that welcomed me. I joined the Liberal Democrats because their core values—tolerance, inclusion, and LGBTQ+ rights—are my values.
I am standing for Brixton Windrush because I believe our neighbourhood deserves better. For too long, the local Council has felt distant and disconnected. After the challenges of Brexit and the ongoing cost-of-living crisis, Brixton is struggling, and the current leadership has been absent when we needed them most.
I bring with me a lifelong passion for community organising, which began in my youth in Rome. I want to bridge the gap between the people of Brixton and the Town Hall.
My priorities for Brixton Windrush are:
• A Present Council: Creating a direct line of communication between residents and decision-makers.
• Equality & Inclusion: Championing the rights of our diverse community and protecting LGBTQ+ spaces.
• Local Recovery: Supporting our high streets and community hubs that have been neglected post-Brexit.
I want to be your voice, listen to your concerns, and be on the frontline to solve them. Let’s build a fairer, more connected Brixton together.

Marco is a local trade union activist, he works at an FE teacher in London. He is a member of the Socialist Party and involved in a campaign locally against councils cuts to children's services and housing. 
Lambeth Labour- led council has already inflicted brutal cuts to services. Now they are planning to make a further £90million! TUSC says, now the hated Tory government is gone, Labour controlled councils have no excuse for making more cuts. Instead councils should use their borrowing powers and reserves to fund the services that people in Lambeth desperately need. Then they should demand the national government picks up the tab. Lambeth has lost £200million in funding since 2010. We want that funding back!

Pedro Xavier is a trilingual language specialist that works closely with the emigrant community in Lambeth.

He was awarded a degree in Bachelor of Arts in Teaching English as a Foreign Language with English Language & British Culture and Communication Studies.

Apart from the work that he now privately does as CEO of PLS Consultants International where he deals with a myriad of problems mostly relating to the language barrier, and runs an accountancy practice.

He worked as an Executive Officer at the DWP, teacher at both the University of Middlesex and Saint Francis Xavier Sixth Form College in London, a Local Education Authority Governor for Bishop Thomas Grant Secondary School and even an external consultant for the Portuguese Consulate in the United Kingdom.

He is also a Portuguese Councillor for the Portuguese communities in the UK, and advisory body to the Portuguese Government

In 2015 Pedro was nominated for the award of “Empreendorismo Inovador na Diáspora Portuguesa” that was given by the Portuguese President, where he was awarded the “Diploma de Mérito”.

He has spent thousands of hours translating and interpreting English, Portuguese and Spanish assisting the minorities within the Borough of Lambeth and has worked with many solicitor firms, both national and foreign, in dealing with a range of issues that arise in the community.

Whenever Pedro is not working at PLS Consultants or patrolling the streets as a Special Constabulary Sergeant at Metropolitan Police he is home spending time with his family and their two dogs

party_name: The Socialist Party of Great Britain
statement_to_voters:
**Socialism: Freedom, Equality, Solidarity**

Socialists are against authoritarianism, privilege and division, We are for freedom, equality, and solidarity. These can only be achieved in a society based on common ownership and democratic control by the whole people.

Freedom means genuine freedom of thought and expression as well as freedom from having to work for wages. We are fully committed to defending open debate and independent thinking. No government or corporation should silence the voices of working people or control how people think and live.

Equality means that every human being is of equal worth regardless of ethnicity, gender, or sexual orientation. We stand for a society of social coexistence based on mutual respect.

Solidarity is the shared commitment to live together with respect, cooperation, and collective responsibility. We stand with working people, defending unity over division and cooperation over conflict.

Capitalism works against these principles. We oppose this economic system that concentrates wealth and power in the hands of a few whilst exploiting the vast majority and that puts profits before people. We oppose the wars it inevitably generates over markets and resources.

We are not standing to manage capitalism locally. If elected, we will be an uncompromising voice for workers in every decision that affects them, making the case for a social revolution to replace capitalism with a society based on common ownership, democratic control, and production directly to meet people’s needs not profits.

Lambeth Labour has let us down. They say there isn't money to keep our streets clean, keep our streetlights bright or to perform basic repairs to housing.

In 2019 I moved to Waterloo and South Bank as a student at King's, and in 2024 I first realised how awful the council was, as I dealt with an 11-month long leak in my flat on a council estate. Sadly, my story is in no way unique.

It's time for a change in Waterloo. Labour can't keep taking us for granted.

Lib Dems run councils better. In Lib Dem-run Sutton, we're building new council homes. Lib Dems listen to residents and work hard for their communities. We stand against division, and we get stuff done. We're problem fixers, not finger-pointers.

Only the Lib Dems can beat Labour in this election, having come just 67 votes behind them in 2022. It would be an honour to serve you as your local councillor, and Doug and I are ready to get stuck in.

Together, we can fix Lambeth.

Like you, I recently received my latest Council Tax bill. Unsurprisingly, it had gone up by just shy of 5%, climbing to over £1,500 a year for a property like mine. Like you, I want our contributions to Lambeth’s finances to provide our area with effective services. In spite of another heavy tax increase, we all know the services on which we all rely will not get better. Instead, under Labour control, Lambeth will continue to deliver poor services. We deserve better.

This by-election is a chance for you to have your say on how Lambeth Labour has run Herne Hill and Loughborough Junction. Talking to people across the ward, there’s a lot to say. Whether you are worried about crime, the fate of local businesses, the condition of your street, how Lambeth has managed your home or the repeated damage to Brockwell Park, you want to send them a message.

Lambeth Labour is not working for Herne Hill and Loughborough Junction. The current Lib Dem and Green opposition on Lambeth Council is not working for Herne Hill and Loughborough Junction. Things will only get worse if the current situation continues. We cannot let that happen.

I’m standing in this by-election because I want things to get better for Herne Hill and Loughborough Junction. I want safer streets, I want local shops to stay open, I want the pavements cleared of weeds and rubbish, I want social tenants not to have to live in mould infested homes and I want to use our parks in the summer. A vote for me as your Conservative candidate is a vote to say you want things to get better, not worse.

Please lend me your vote on 1st May.

I am delighted to be the Labour Candidate for my home ward of Weetwood. I am excited to work alongside Cllr Emma Flint who has set such a high standard and is bring about real change and our MP Alex Sobel who is delivering for our community.  I work for a local debt advice charity on the frontline of the cost of living. You'll always be able to reach me and you will see me around Weetwood, whether it's on a community walk, collecting donations for the food bank, or supporting a community project.

Last year your vote made a difference Labour Councillor Emma Flint. We're both local residents and care about the area.

Nadja is a designer who has lived in Lewisham for nearly a decade.

Having grown up in social housing, Nadja is committed to advocating for a higher standard of living for everyone, especially those in rented accommodation. She is standing to ensure Bellingham receives the attention, investment, and representation it deserves.

Nadja believes that big changes come from small, consistent steps, starting at the community level. Local council decisions should be guided by those who live in the area, as they understand its needs best and should have agency over their own communities.

Ade has lived in Brockley for three years, where her life in Lewisham began. She is passionate about ensuring communities across Lewisham win together, and believes that’s the Green Party way.

By day, Ade works in the tech industry and also serves as a director of a CIC exploring the impact of data-centric technologies on Black people and other marginalised groups. Deeply invested in the local community,

“I am invested in making Brockley, and Lewisham as a whole, affordable and sustainable enough that others who love it as much as I do, will be able to stay and continue to make it the amazing place it is.”

Rashid has been a Lewisham resident for 27 years and has seen first-hand how the borough has been let down by decades of neglect and underinvestment. Growing up on a council estate and being given the opportunity of higher education shaped his belief that everyone, regardless of background, deserves the chance to thrive.

“I’m standing because I want to serve my community, my neighbours who, like me, have been let down by our political class.”

Miki was born in London and moved to Lewisham 12 years ago, where she has been happily based ever since.

She is passionate about tackling air pollution, having been diagnosed with asthma and advised by her GP that it was linked to traffic in London. She is particularly concerned about issues such as motor idling and wider environmental and social challenges affecting local communities.

Miki is an enthusiastic cyclist and runner, and works as a theatre technician. She is committed to helping create a healthier, fairer environment for everyone in Lewisham.

Hau-Yu was the first London councillor to defect to the Greens in 2025, after facing political persecution for standing up for Palestine.

She saw up close Labour’s embrace of racism, fascism, and transphobia – growth with zero wellbeing or social justice indicators, fuelling genocide and war. The Greens stood by her, and now she’s fighting back against those injustices.

As an anti-oppression campaigner, Hau-Yu cares about protecting and empowering our communities. She’ll listen to you and fight to support Deptford. We’re in this together.

Her family are migrants from Hong Kong, China and Malaysia – some have lived in Lewisham since the 90s. This campaign is for all of us.

Sylbourne came to the UK from Jamaica in 1992 and has spent the past three decades building a life, a family, and a voice for his community in Lewisham.

An entrepreneur, lawyer, content creator and motivational speaker, Sylbourne has dedicated his career to inspiring people, connecting communities, and being a catalyst for change. He founded the “Politics and You” initiative to educate and empower young people to engage with political decision-making — because, as he believes, the leaders of tomorrow must be equipped to shape their future today.

In 2015, Sylbourne launched the Sylbourne Sydial Show, a thought-provoking talk show showcasing ordinary people doing extraordinary things. During the Covid pandemic, he pioneered “Walk & Talk Motivational Moments”, going live daily from the park to encourage people to stay positive and keep moving forward.

Proudly married with two children, Sylbourne also serves on the board of his local church.

His priorities for Grove Park are clear:

-Safer, cleaner streets where residents feel proud and secure.
    -Support for young people, giving them the skills and confidence to succeed.
    -Revitalising our high street, backing small businesses and driving regeneration.
    -A stronger voice for our community on Lewisham Council.

“I consider my life a platform — unapologetic in its values, determined to connect people, and driven to make a difference,” Sylbourne said. “It would be my honour to represent Grove Park.”

Eleonora emigrated from Italy to London in 2020. She did a master’s in education policy, worked in secondary schools as support staff, and is now a research student in education and youth well-being at King’s College – where she also teaches.

Eleonora is standing for the Greens in Hither Green because in them she found a like-minded community of people whose actions reflect care about socio-economic justice for all.

Ese Erheriene grew up in a working-class household in inner city London. From there she learned hard work, drive and compassion. The daughter of Nigerian immigrants, she also learned that Labour was the only party that could be relied upon.

She is standing to be Woking's first Labour MP because it is a town full of hardworking people who deserve to have their voices heard in the halls of Westminster. She cares deeply about the town and its communities --- and she wants to use her experience to help address the areas where Woking has been failed by 14 years of Tory austerity, and to champion the areas where it is leading the way.

Labour values have driven her throughout her career and led her to becoming a financial journalist, where she uncovered financial corruption and wrote about pro-democracy movements.

As a board member for a mental health charity, she developed a peer support programme during the pandemic that supported vulnerable people and helped them find community.

As a local councillor, she has a strong track record of being a voice for the voiceless. She brought ThamesWater executives before the committee to demand that the cost of their spiralling debts not be passed onto consumers.

She has continually fought to resolve complex casework, ranging from meeting emergency housing needs, to supporting vulnerable residents with mobility issues, to tackling serious breaches of environmental regulations. She is currently a cabinet advisor with the portfolio of improving health inequalities for women, through which she has called on the government to launch a full statutory enquiry into maternity safety.

And during the Cost of Living Crisis, Ese raised hundreds of pounds for people struggling to heat their homes by trekking to Everest Base Camp.

Born in the Dominican Republic, Wellington is a Spanish citizen who has lived in London for 11 years, 8 of them in Lewisham.

As a Latin American and first-generation immigrant, he’s intimately familiar with the challenges our community faces: council negligence, tenant abuses, and lack of support for social housing and young people with disabilities like his daughter.

“I want to be the voice of the Latin American community in Lewisham, advocating for rights, improving access to services, promoting Spanish in schools, fostering Latin culture, health, employment, and transparency.”

Originally from Canada, Markus has lived in Sydenham for 12 years. He’s passionate about collective people power and community, particularly how it can bring about real change. He knows how important it is for people to be involved in the way their local community is organised and fostered.

A musician and teacher, Markus has curated and hosted a community music night on the high street for the past four years, been involved with the Sydenham Arts Trail, and supported other local events throughout Sydenham and the wider area.

He plans to improve green spaces, access to local services, and social housing. He wants to help make Sydenham & Lewisham a safe place for everyone.

“I’m running because I love Sydenham and the people here. I want to make sure that our community is fully represented and that everyone’s voices are heard. I’ve spent years writing songs about hope, and I believe that with the Green Party, I can help bring those hopes to life.”

I’m a North West-born artist and community organiser with Nigerian roots, now rooted in Northenden for nearly nine of my 21 years in Manchester.

My work centres on bringing people together; championing those too often pushed to the margins, helping communities find their voice. I’m co-director of Rainbow Noir, have worked across film and TV, served on the Contact Theatre board, and delivered cultural competency training for the NHS, Home Office, Greater Manchester Combined Authority and Fire and Rescue Service. I can influence decision-makers, but I’m still someone you could have a pint with at The Farmer’s.

After serving as a member of Youth Parliament in my teens I swore off party politics for good, let down by a system built for and by the few.

The Green Party changed that. I’m standing because Northenden deserves practical, hopeful, community-rooted politics that turns our differences into strengths.

I joined the Green Party in early 2025 and quickly began creating content focused on raising awareness and solidarity around injustice across the world, from Gaza to Ukraine.

I soon gained experience as a campaign manager, before being inspired to put myself forward as a candidate following the Bournemouth party conference.

As a representative for Muslim Greens, I’m particularly keen to engage younger generations and members of the Asian community across Manchester. I was honoured to be awarded a Heraldic Badge by the Dame Robina Shah Foundation for service to young people’s education in February, presented by the Lord Mayor of Manchester.

As a candidate, I’m strongly committed to championing justice and integrity above all else, ensuring that my friends and neighbours across Whalley Range and beyond feel seen, heard and supported.

I moved to Merton almost 20 years ago and immediately felt a connection with the green spaces and wonderful diversity in the area. We are blessed to have an incredible 115 parks and green spaces in our borough.
One of the defining characteristics of Merton is its strong sense of community. However, despite these positives, we know Merton also comes with a number of challenges. If elected as councillor I will robustly challenge the council on issues such as:
•	Housing - Merton has seen too many cases of unsafe private rentals and weak enforcement. I will push for stronger action against rogue landlords, better insulation standards, and real support for tenants.

•	Fly-Tipping – commitment to work with the council to root out the worst offenders and push for maximum fines etc.

•	Questioning the delays to the £30M regeneration project of Morden – the people of Morden deserve better!

•	Highlighting Anti-social behaviour, pushing for more social housing, challenging poor practice in social care and making sure our open spaces are respected and kept clean will be some of the issues I am committed to.

•	I will not remain silent if I see poor council practice – this will be challenged.

On international issues, I can say firmly that I oppose the ongoing bombing of Gaza and Lebanon, which many international organisations and human‑rights groups have described as unlawful or disproportionate. I believe Merton should be a borough that stands for peace, human rights, and the protection of civilians everywhere. These values guide how I approach local issues too — fairness, accountability, and compassion.
In thanking you for taking the time to read this is would ask for your support to help Merton Greens deliver our shared aims - a better (Merton/Colliers Wood) for us all.

I have been a life-long campaigner for the environment and social justice: I set up a branch of Friends of the Earth 45 years ago. At Warwick University I was tutored by a postgraduate student by the name of Nick Stern, now Professor Lord Stern and author of the crucial Stern Review on the Economics of Climate Change. Now that I approach retirement, I am keen to direct my time towards bringing about the huge local changes that we need to make in order to keep Merton safe and prosperous in the face of climactic environmental breakdown. I co-lead on Transport in Merton’s Climate Action Group and led its precursor in the Climate Emergency Working Group as well as actively contribute to ad hoc local pressure groups such as Merton Tree Keepers, Hold Merton To Account (on poor planning decisions) and others.  Disability is a special area of interest: my son's complex needs taught me to understand the huge range of issues faced by those with limited mobility or complex needs. 

In recent years I applied my technical knowledge and skills to bring transformative change to major local authorities, and I believe that there are opportunities to have far better joined-up services here in Merton too. If elected, I will press to redirect Merton's plans and policies towards achieving a model community that attracts and retains the people and new kinds of work that will thrive in meeting the challenges that will overwhelm a less well prepared borough.

I was born in Dagenham and have lived all over London, but Merton has been my home since 2008. I care deeply about this borough and am passionate about its rich diversity, beautiful green spaces, and sense of community. Although there is a lot that is already great about Merton, there is so much more we can do to ensure that we all have the same opportunities, and to build a borough that brings us together rather than divides us.

I spent 24 years in central government at the Foreign Office, including serving as an Ambassador. Working around the world showed me the difference that good, responsive local government can make to people’s lives. Since leaving the Civil Service, I have put my energy into helping build stronger communities at home, including through volunteering with several local groups and as a charity trustee. I now want to bring that same energy to Council.

I am standing for Council because Merton deserves leadership that listens to residents, reflects our diversity, and acts on what matters most. I will fight for equal opportunities, strong local services, and real action on the cost‑of‑living crisis so that everyone in our borough can thrive.

I can see a greener, more equitable future on the horizon. With my work in grassroots circular economy projects, and with the huge positive impact that volunteering at a local community orchard has had on my life, I feel so much hope for what green, sustainable policies can do to improve people's lives.

Originally from North East England, I have lived in Mitcham (Graveney Ward) since 2018. I’m very proud to call this friendly and diverse community my home. London can be isolating for those of us who moved here from elsewhere, but being involved in grassroots environmental projects in Mitcham allowed me to connect with neighbours. Together we make our neighbourhoods greener, more welcoming, and supportive.
 
I feel very strongly that standing up against environmental harms and standing up against the harms of discrimination and other social injustices are part of the same fight. I have worked in the charity sector for over 10 years, and in both my professional and personal life I have advocated for positive change for people and the planet through evidence-based advocacy, policy development, communications, campaigns, and community organising. This has included working with local councils across England to implement safe access zones outside abortion clinics to prevent the harassment of patients accessing care.

I am a regular volunteer at Mitcham Community Orchard, having gained a qualification in community orchard management. I share my passion for ecological gardening with others, delivering community workshops on orchard care. Our beautiful community orchard has been awarded Level 5 (Outstanding) two years in a row at the RHS London in Bloom awards' "It's Your Neighbourhood" category.

I have also worked for local charity Sustainable Merton, managing their waste reduction and circular economy project to offer free community repair and upcycling events across Mitcham alongside a team of skilled volunteers. I hope to be a strong voice for our volunteering community who give so much to our area.

The environmental and social damage caused by illegal fly tipping and littering in Mitcham is an ongoing blight on our neighbourhood, which has driven me to litter pick my local area for many years. I believe reducing pollution caused by littering is a huge part of community care, and something we can all engage in.

I was part of the voluntary Climate Emergency Working Group assisting Merton Council to develop their Climate Action Plan and Strategy in 2019, but I fear the momentum on climate action has waned. It is time to make the changes that will benefit our communities and our environment.

I’m proud to stand as a candidate for Merton – the community I’ve called home all my life.

I grew up here, I’ve raised my family here, and like so many of you, I care deeply about our borough and its future.

My journey has always been about standing up for people. From representing workers who lost their livelihoods in 1991, to creating Merton’s first local jobs board, I’ve worked to open up opportunities and support those who need it most.

More recently, I’ve been campaigning for a stronger local democracy. Residents deserve to be heard properly – with real debate, real accountability, and real action.

I’m also proud to be part of our local community, from supporting AFC Wimbledon, to helping grassroots campaigns that brought people together to achieve something bigger.

At my core, I believe something simple: everyone matters.

I’m standing because Merton deserves leadership that listens, acts, and stands up for its people.

I have lived in Morden for over ten years and really appreciate the benefits and advantages of living in such a great place – good transport links, a dynamic, thriving town centre, and easy access to lots of wide-open green spaces.

Having been in the IT industry for over 20 years and, before that, working on the Lombard Trading Estate in South Wimbledon, I have witnessed the changing nature of Merton over that time and feel proud to live in the area.

Before moving to Morden, I grew up in Southfields, next to Wimbledon Park, which gave me my lifelong love of parks and was a factor in me moving to Morden.

I feel that parks are one of our most valuable assets we have as a society and should be treasured for all the health and social benefits they bring us, and I try to use them as much as possible and encourage others to do so as well.

I have decided to stand as a Councillor because I am a passionate believer in giving back to society, as demonstrated by my volunteer work with various local groups, and the Green Party is the natural home for my sense of social inclusion, peace and justice.

I’m standing to serve the Pollards Hill community and the wider Merton area.

I care deeply about issues affecting everyday life in Pollards Hill and the wider borough, including affordable housing, clean air, safer streets, and better public services. I believe local politics should be rooted in listening to residents and acting on what matters most to them.

If elected, I would work to ensure that community voices are central to decision-making, and that environmental responsibility goes hand in hand with social justice.

Real change begins when we stop speaking about people and start listening to them. Leadership is not about speaking louder, it is about understanding more deeply, and acting when it matters most.

Politics should not be about promises made from a distance, but about decisions grounded in the real lives of the people they affect.

“The monster of tyranny has masqueraded in the garb of democracy, but people are led to think that it is the goddess of freedom.” – Sir Muhammad Iqbal
I am an NHS clinician and local community champion with two decades of roots in Milton Keynes. I am standing for the Liberal Democrats to continue my lifelong commitment to public service.

**Building a Better Bradwell for Everyone**

I have called Milton Keynes home for the last 8 years. Like many of you, I moved here for the opportunity, the community, and the chance to build a bright future for my family. As a software professional and a business owner, I believe in looking at problems logically and finding efficient, common-sense solutions.
I am standing for the local council because I believe that local government should serve every resident equally. Whether you have lived here for decades or have recently made Bradwell your home, your concerns are my priorities.

**My Commitment to You:**

*Safe & Accessible Streets:* From fixing potholes to improving lighting, I will ensure our infrastructure is safe and reliable for all commuters, parents, and elderly residents.

*Fiscal Accountability:* I am committed to ensuring your council tax is spent wisely on the services that actually matter to our ward, cutting out waste and bureaucracy.

*A United Community:* I will be a visible, approachable representative who listens to every voice, ensuring that our diverse neighbourhood is a place where everyone feels they belong and are heard.

*Support for Local Families:* I will advocate for better local amenities and cleaner, greener public spaces for all generations to enjoy.

*I am asking for your vote.* I don’t just want to represent a ward; I want to represent our shared aspiration for a safer, more prosperous, and well-managed community.

🗳️ Election Information
Candidate: KOYADA, Gowri Shankar
Ward: Bradwell Ward
Date: Thursday, 7th May

**Let’s work together for a stronger Bradwell. Every vote counts!**

**About**

I was born in Kenya and moved to Milton Keynes as a child in 1976, when the city was still in its earliest stages of development. Growing up alongside Milton Keynes, I’ve seen first-hand how our community has evolved—and I even played a small part in its story, helping to create one of our famous concrete cows.

My career has been rooted in community and creativity. Throughout the 1980s and 90s, I worked to support and develop the arts in Milton Keynes, alongside involvement in youth work, counselling, and initiatives tackling homelessness across our city.

Coming from a family background in human rights and constitutional law, I’ve always believed in putting people at the heart of decision-making. I am committed to bringing that human-centred approach into local government—advocating for better council services, fairer planning, and housing that truly meets the needs of residents.

The pandemic revealed both the strength of our communities and the deep inequalities that persist. Like many, I became disillusioned with everyday politics—but that changed when I heard Zack Polanski speak. It renewed my sense of hope and purpose, and inspired me to stand as a local candidate to help turn that hope into real change for Milton Keynes.

**Campaign Priorities**

Milton Keynes’ greatest strength is its diversity—of culture, ideas, and people. As your councillor, I will work to harness these strengths to build a fairer, more inclusive city.

I will stand up to the negative impacts of developer-led housing by pushing for genuinely affordable homes and protecting the integrity of our communities. Tackling inequality—locally and across the city—will be central to everything I do, alongside improving how residents access and experience council services to ensure better outcomes for everyone, especially those most marginalised.

I am passionate about creating more opportunities for young people. By supporting arts, sports, and community initiatives—and working closely with local schools and organisations—I want to help young residents thrive and feel connected to their community.

Protecting our green spaces is also vital. Milton Keynes’ parks and lakes are essential to our wellbeing and identity. I will work with Milton Keynes City Council and The Parks Trust to safeguard these spaces, ensuring they remain healthy, accessible, and sustainable for future generations.

Above all, I believe in the power of community. Together, we can shape a city that reflects our shared values—fairness, opportunity, and care for one another. Let’s make hope something we live by every day, and work collectively to create a Milton Keynes we are all proud to call home.

Uroy has lived in Milton Keynes for almost 40 years and has served as a Councillor for 11 years, currently representing Middleton and Oakgrove. Alongside his public service, he is the Cross-Campus Cluster Lead at UEL and Chair of City College London.

Locally, Uroy is a strong advocate for improving outcomes for vulnerable children. He is campaigning for more high-quality children’s homes for those in care, alongside better mental health support services. He believes that local people should be better supported to provide these services, rather than relying heavily on large external corporations.

Uroy works closely with community groups and residents and has tackled a wide range of local issues and addressing everyday concerns such as potholes, street lighting, graffiti, and fly-tipping.

Beyond his council work, Uroy is actively involved in his local church and is passionate about mentoring and supporting young people. As Shadow Cabinet Member for Equalities, Leisure and Culture, he has championed inclusive community initiatives and supported campaigns such as White Ribbon, promoting the protection and safety of women.

Macsene has lived in Milton Keynes for over 20 years. She is married with a teenage daughter, and works as a Process Engineering Consultant in the energy sector where she designs “green” technology add-ons for existing facilities.

She has been a Parish Councillor in Broughton and Milton Keynes Village Parish for over 5 years, where she works to help young people thrive.  This year she has helped to secure funding and access to mental health for young people in the Parish. She looks forward to helping young people across the ward as a city councillor.

She wants to work with residents of all ages to improve life in the community and has met with people at local warm spaces, parish events, litter picking and other community events. She has worked with Oakgrove School to reduce litter along the H7, and got streetlights and potholes fixed and graffiti, litter and fly-tipping removed from across Ouzel Valley ward.

In her spare time she likes to read and is trying to write a novel.

As a husband and father, I’ve lived and worked in Buckinghamshire for over a decade. I’m standing to keep Ouzel Valley safe, clean, and thriving, supporting local businesses, protecting our countryside, and delivering practical solutions for residents.

Khadija is a proud student at Northumbria University and a young person who believes that our community deserves positive leadership, opportunity, and unity. She is standing as a local councillor to give back to the city that has shaped her education and future. She understands the pressures facing students, families, and working people, from housing and safety to jobs and inclusion. She wants to help build a community where everyone feels heard, supported, and respected.

Will is standing for election in Byker because he cares deeply about supporting our community and has a passion for helping those in need. He is committed to listening, working hard and standing up for the people of Byker and the issues they are facing.

I am standing because our community deserves **a strong voice** that truly listens to residents and works for our **long-term good.** Through my work with the Great Park Neighbourhood Association, I have worked alongside residents **to raise local concerns, support community activities and bring people together to address issues** that matter to us all.

Like many residents, I care deeply about the future of our area. Castle Ward is a fantastic place to live, with vibrant communities, beautiful green spaces and families who want the very best for their neighbourhood. However, many residents feel their concerns are not always properly heard. Local decisions can sometimes feel distant from the people they affect most. That is why I have decided to stand for election.

I am not a career politician. I am someone **already working** alongside residents to improve our neighbourhood. If elected, I will represent Castle Ward with **integrity, energy, and commitment.** I would be grateful for your support.

As an NHS worker and active member of UNISON, Marley sees every day how vital strong public services are, and what happens when they are underfunded. She is committed to protecting and investing in our NHS, social care, youth services, and local amenities so they truly serve our community. She has consistently campaigned for low-paid workers to be paid the real Living Wage and have worked to strengthen workplace protections, including supporting the implementation of the Employment Rights Act. As a young worker, she is passionate about improving the voice and representation of young people in decision-making. Marley believes issues such as sustainability, clean and healthy environments are fundamental fair and thriving communities. If elected, she will stand up for fair pay, fair funding for strong public services, and a council that works for everyone.

Killian has been a resident of Jesmond for Nearly 20 years and cares deeply about the area. From supporting our nature resources in the wonderful Jesmond Dene to the Town Moor. Building and maintaining relationships with the Freemen of Newcastle and continuing to protect Jesmond Dene are essential. As a teacher for 20 years, he understand the need to build positive partnerships with residents and stakeholders. As a Run Director at Town Moor parkrun, he sees thousands of residents and tourists come visit us in Jesmond and is proud to showcase and promote our city and Jesmond itself. Killian wants to protect residents’ streets as well – with quick resolution of potholes and litter in our streets and back lanes, but also protecting the quality of rental housing in the area.

I am Ludovico Rella, I am an Assistant Professor at Durham University researching the impact of Artificial Intelligence on society. I moved to the North East from Italy ten years ago anc I fell in love with Newcastle, its history, its culture and its values.

I believe in public, affordable and accessible higher education and in a sustainable and constructive relationship between universities and the local community. I want to contribute to bringing students and local residents together in Jesmond to build a stronger and more inclusive community, and to ensure affordability and quality of rental properties.

I stand as a Green candidate not only to make sure that divisive and hateful politics stay out of power in Newcastle, but also to contribute making our city a positive example for progressive, equitable and sustainable policies that can inspire the rest of the UK.

Finley is a Law student at Newcastle University with a strong interest in justice, public service, and community representation. He is actively involved in politics and is committed to ensuring that the voices of those living in Jesmond are properly heard. He believes both long-term residents and students deserve fair representation and a strong local voice. If elected, Finley will work hard to deliver for Jesmond and put residents at the heart of every decision. His priorities include improving housing standards, tackling the cost of living, and protecting Jesmond’s environment and community character.

Gordana has lived in Kingston Park for 20 years and is familiar with local issues. She has worked to protect green spaces, for clean air in the face of increasing traffic congestion, and has helped get funding for play area improvement in Kingston Park. She has a special interest in improving public transport and interconnectivity, and is keen to bring her energy to making a real difference to the local community.

I've decided to step forward because I truly believe our infrastructure is not working as it should. Too many things are falling short, and the policies meant to support people aren't always delivering.

My background is in business, management and charity work, where I've worked with people from all walks of life. This has given me a clear understanding of the challenges many in our community face.

I believe politics should bring people together, not divide us or leave anyone behind. We need practical solutions that work for everyone.
I'm standing because I care about our community and want to be a voice for positive, real change.

Ali has proudly called Newcastle his home for the past 23 years. He holds a PhD and currently works with Santander, His greatest passion has always been serving and strengthening our community. As Vice-Chair of an anti-radicalisation initiative, he has worked closely with families and young people to promote integration and help keep our youth away from extremism. Ali cares deeply about our community which is why he has decided to stand as a local councillor — to help make our neighbourhood safer, improve quality of life, break down barriers between people, and bring communities together in peace and unity. He will stand with residents, listen to them, and work tirelessly to support our community whenever needed.

Hi! I'm Chuck Taylor. From the first moment me and my wife visited Walker we could feel its warmth and community spirit, and that's why we've made it our home.

But after living here for only a short time it became clear it was a community that was being ignored, a community that had been taken for granted, a community that had been let down.

Then I had a knock on the door from the Green Party, asking to hear my thoughts and concerns as a local and I knew that someone was listening.

So I joined them in their work cleaning up our streets and green spaces, and knocking on doors to listen to the concerns of people round here.
Now I am determined to give those people a voice. Vote Green on May 7th, and I'll make sure that the people of Walker's voices are finally heard.

Brad has lived on the same street in Walkergate his entire life. He attended school in the area at St. Albans Roman Catholic Primary School, has been involved with the local Football Club Newcastle East End FC and built further ties with the community through his caseworker role for our local Member of Parliament. Working for one of our local Labour MPs has allowed him to support residents in our area with a multitude of issues, ranging from their housing or benefits, to more local issues such as anti-social behaviour or cleaning up our spaces.

Mariadas is a qualify electrical engineer works on building services project over 40 years Even though you may not have considered voting Conservative before, by lending us your support together we can ensure Newham plastow north has councillor serving local communities needs on social housing, social care, youth education, employment, street security, street traffic control, street cleaning, prevention of anti social behaviour, prevent street crime, good neighbouring communities protection and working towards low emission clean environments. Newham labour council failure more  than 30 years, on local communities needs. Vote conservative party candidate on 5/05/2022.

I’m Taila, and I’m standing in the upcoming County Council election for the Attleborough Division.

I was born and raised in Attleborough, and I’ve been proud to serve our community for over 10 years — currently as Deputy Mayor and as a Breckland District Councillor for the Attleborough Burgh & Haverscroft Ward. Public service runs in my family, and I’m proud to be the third generation to serve our town.

My biggest inspiration is my daughter, Paisley. As a mum to an autistic child, I understand how challenging it can be for families to access the right support, which is why improving SEND services and building a kinder, more inclusive community means so much to me.

I’m a councillor who believes in doing the right thing — not the easy thing. I’ve always stepped up when our town needed it, whether it was organising community support during the pandemic or helping residents directly during the 2023 floods. I care deeply about this place and the people in it, and I’ll always roll up my sleeves when needed.

My priorities:

• Making sure Attleborough gets the infrastructure it needs as we grow

• Improving SEND support for children, young people and adults

• Working to reduce flood risk in Attleborough and Besthorpe

• Ensuring public money is used wisely and effectively

If I’m elected, you’ll have a strong, consistent voice representing you across all three levels of local government — someone who will always listen and always fight for what’s best for our community, championing the needs of Attleborough and Besthorpe every step of the way.

"Right now feels like a time where people are looking for change, and I see that as an opportunity to do things a bit differently at a local level. I’m particularly focused on the everyday things that make a real difference to people’s lives.

I also care about supporting vulnerable people. How we look after those who need support, including older members of our community, says a lot about who we are.

Growing up in the area gave me a strong sense of community. We know we have something good here, and we want to keep it that way. What unites us all is a genuine care for our community, our environment and each other, and that is something I am passionate about building on."

Having lived here for 5 years and been a regular contributor to The Diss Express and Park Radio I also had my own record shop in Diss for three years. I was part of a community shop No 8 Market place where I felt the real spirit and voice of the town. Last summer I introduced people from the Market to Adrian Ramsey and following the GE result realised there is genuine hope out there. I do owe some of enthusiasm to speaking with people to the Lib Dem’s. I campaigned on council by-elections and national ones also. My good friend Bobby Dean is now an MP, Bobby and I worked closely way back around Brexit.

I see the enthusiasm but enormous struggle within our community to find a positive narrative. I would love to see more investment and time donations put into filling empty shops in Diss with active projects that help relieve the strain put on single parents and families that will soon be taking on the heavy lifting set by Labour’s budget. I feel that Zack’s words can be echoed down into every single community. I would like to see the basics tackled head on, such as ways of tackling climate change in micro actions and bigger ones such as a 20 MPH limit throughout Diss and Roydon. I would like to see post school clubs for children of all ages that look to educate and encourage different practical learning skills. I would love to see local businesses brought in to help donate to these causes but willingly and proudly. An example would be the now empty Aldi which presumably will be empty for months or years to come. I would also like to try and turn heads towards a need for community run entertainment hubs like a cinema.

What excites me is the prospect of people rejecting hate speech from Reform and looking towards what they can do within their community to both help themselves and their town or village. I think once people see Green action within their community they’ll be far more likely to then take on simple acts that help the environment. I would love to be part of taking on a role that helped push the Green agenda but also work with people that want to help. I feel being honest with people about wind farms and solar fields, helping people and pushing for fairer placements of greener energy sources such as car parks, new housing developments.

I love speaking with people and I want to find a solution on a micro level to the real threat Diss and Roydon faces. From flags to the food banks or the litter we see clogging up the countryside. I’m enthusiastic and absolutely ready to get started. I would really like to build a fun and willing team around me that galvanises the new and old members. Being as I joined the party recently I feel I have a lot in common with the new surge and vibrancy of those people and a willingness to learn from others.

I’m Jaden Lister, A member of Reform UK from Norfolk.

I stepped up because, as a young man, I’m concerned about the direction our country is heading. Too many people no longer feel safe or proud of the place they call home and that isn’t right.

We can do better. Safer streets, stronger communities, and a country people can believe in again. 🇬🇧

Krissi has lived in Bedworth for 18 years. She is married with 3 children, who have all attended local schools, with the eldest now in the local Sixth Form.   Krissi has more than 20 years of retail experience, including 6 years working in the newsagents in town. She now runs her own pet shop business on Bedworth market, which enable her to combine work with her passion for animals.  Being a business owner and trader in Bedworth, Krissi has the both the business skills and people skills that would make her an excellent councillor for Bede ward.   One of her children is disabled and so she has experience of what life is like when you have a child with additional needs  She is a member of the Warwickshire Parent Carer Voice.

Tariq Saeed (any pronouns) is a born-and-raised 'Bartoner' who grew up in Barton before studying at the University of Oxford, which they graduated from in 2025 — as a student activist he campaigned for ethnic minority, LGBTQ+, and working-class students.

Tariq is passionate about addressing the challenges facing the environment and understands that our solutions must also benefit everyday working people - he believes “only a party not funded by billionaires and fossil fuel giants can reliably stand up for people AND the planet.”

Indigo Haynes (she/her) is a modern languages student in her final year at Oxford University, with plans to start a teacher training course in Oxford after graduating.

She's been involved with local politics since she was a teenager, having run campaigns in her hometown to save a local hospital from shutdown, get better bus routes with cheaper fares, and led climate change protests and 'Fridays For Future' school walk-outs.

She was also a member of Youth Parliament, and secretary of her local Labour Party; since Starmer's election as Prime Minister, she left the party, feeling betrayed by his government's actions. Now in Oxford, her focus is on the same issues she's always fought for: preserving local services, improving public transport, and working to fix the climate crisis.

Hi! I’m Awab Kennedy, a student at Teddy Hall! That means that I understand what it is that students need most: better quality housing that doesn’t cost a fortune, or that you have to queue for overnight, a more vibrant nighttime economy, and real action on the climate crisis. That’s why I’m standing to be your next city councillor, to ensure that you have a real voice on the things that matter most to you. If that’s something that appeals to you, then make sure to vote Labour on the 7th of May!

As an independent City Council candidate for Rose Hill and Iffley, I’m deeply rooted in this ward where my parents have lived for 40 years.

I’ve been a local resident and entrepreneur running local businesses for over a decade. I understand the economic  pressures faced by residents and local businesses. While I champion sustainability through my work in the EV sector, I believe transport must be practical. I’m passionate about removing LTNs and reviewing road speeds to resolve the severe congestion and keep Oxford’s transport flowing smoothly for everyone.

I also stand for global human rights and as a pro-Palestine voice for the community. I have worked in Local Government for many years . The role of Local Government is to help local people not making life more difficult. My whole family has voted for Labour for many years , Labour have let us down and we need change.

As an independent councillor, my commitment is to listen to your concerns and deliver common-sense leadership and pragmatic solutions for our community.

Zelalemawee Asheber (he/him) is running for Walton Manor because he wants to ensure, by implementing the Green manifesto, that not only does Walton Manor remain a beautiful place to live but becomes more affordable too.

Zelalemawee is currently doing a PhD in antibiotic resistance research at the University of Oxford. He pursued this work because of a long-term motivation to help others that is derived from a gratitude for the selfless care of his mother and an awareness of the current unmet needs of people in this country and abroad.

By working to improve those inadequate conditions, it allows him to do as Sylvia Pankhurst wrote: ‘It came to me as a challenge: seek to obtain for others the care you had.’

For the exact same reason, he wants to work just as hard for his local area, Walton Manor.

Cristina Palamini (she/they) is standing as a Green Party candidate because she believes Oxford has the talent and resources to be a greener, fairer city — and that what’s needed now is the political will to act. She cares deeply about Oxford as a place to live, work and build a future, and believes local politics must be rooted in fairness, sustainability and long-term thinking.

She has lived in Oxford for five years. As an immigrant to the UK, she was drawn by the openness of the community and is committed to keeping Oxford a city where people from all backgrounds can belong and build secure, fulfilling lives.

She has been politically active throughout her life as an activist, motivated by climate action and social justice. As an Italian, she brings a strong awareness of the risks of complacency in the face of extremism and believes Oxford needs bold leadership to address the climate emergency, the housing crisis and the cost of living.

She works as Chief Product Officer at an education technology start-up focused on widening access to learning, bringing a commitment to practical, evidence-based solutions alongside the ambition needed to deliver real change."

Working, living and supporting a breadth of voluntary organisations provides me with a wide range of experience, knowledge and understanding of the local issues and needs of the people of Cosham. As a champion of community engagement, I wants to use my skills and experience to benefit the residents of Cosham and make living here good for everyone.

Kristine works in the beauty industry in North End and is soon to become the mother of her first child.

She is an active local campaigner and supports the Hands Off Portsmouth campaign.

Having lived locally for a decade, I’m standing as a councillor candidate to help shape a positive future. With a career in marketing, property and asset management, I will support residents, strengthen services, and back local businesses—ensuring our community continues to grow, prosper, and offer opportunities for everyone.

Ulrike has lived by Prospect Park for nearly 20 years, where she is raising her family. She works as a Project Manager for a local business in Reading and understands the importance of listening and acting on issues and concerns. 
Ulrike volunteers in local youth sports and is passionate about giving children equal opportunities in life.  
If elected she will prioritise issues that matter most to Southcote residents:
continue to report fly tipping and littering, work with the police and the Council to tackle  anti-social behaviour, report pot holes and protect Southcote’s green spaces.

As a long-term Aldborough resident, I care about safer streets, cleaner neighbourhoods, better roads, and strong local services. I want to support practical improvements, listen to residents, and help ensure our community remains a great place to live for everyone.

I have lived in Redbridge since I was 16, including 20 years in South Woodford and since 2000 in Barkingside. My children attended local schools and I volunteer with the Air Cadets. I run a training company and IT rental business, volunteer at a food bank, and will fight over development, traffic problems, and give residents a stronger voice.

I have lived in Redbridge for 55 years and reside within the Bridge Ward. My wife and I raised two children here and I served as a magistrate for over a decade. As such I know the harsh impact of crime in our neighbourhood.

I believe Reform UK will work hard to reduce crime, build houses, repair roads and invest in the locality. I stand for local issues.

Trust Reform UK. Vote Vasis.

statement_to_voters:
Redbridge resident of 12 years, I’m standing to protect Hainault’s green spaces, including Brocket Way, tackle crime and cleanliness, and address concerns near Manford Way.

I will ensure residents, not top-down decisions, shape our ward’s future.

With over 20 years’ experience, I provide strong, accountable leadership and deliver the results we need.

party_description_text: Redbridge Matters: Independent Voice for Change

statement_to_voters:
I have lived in Redbridge since 2009 and in Newbury since 2017. As a local father and IT professional with 15 years of problem-solving experience, I am standing to ensure our ward gets the attention it deserves.

My priorities for Newbury:
Safer Streets: Tackling crime and improving road safety.
Cleaner Neighbourhoods: Fixing fly-tipping and maintaining our local environment.
Better Services: Demanding transparency and accountability in how your council tax is spent.

I am not a career politician—I am a local resident who wants to listen to you and deliver practical solutions for our community.

Hi, I’m Imran. I’ve lived in Redbridge for 25 years and have been a doctor for 20 years, worked locally as a GP for 7 years, supporting patients through local hospitals, GP surgeries and out-of-hours services. I’m passionate about improving healthcare, ensuring safe streets and reviewing overdevelopment in our area. I want to work with residents to build a healthier, safer and stronger community for everyone.

Hi, I’m Kes. I’ve lived in Seven Kings since 2009 and have worked in the NHS while also serving as a foster carer. These experiences have given me a strong understanding of the pressures facing local families. My priorities are tackling overdevelopment, improving street cleanliness and safety, and investing in youth services and opportunities. I believe Seven Kings deserves strong local representation that listens to residents and focuses on practical solutions that improve everyday life.

party_description_text: Redbridge Matters: Independent Voice for Change

statement_to_voters: Hi, I’m Rayhana. I’ve lived and worked in Redbridge for 11 years as a local GP, caring for residents and understanding the challenges families face. As a mother with children in local schools, I care deeply about our community and its future. My priorities are affordable housing, protecting vital local services and ensuring our streets and public spaces are safe and welcoming. I want to bring compassion, practical experience and a strong community voice to represent residents and help build a healthier, safer and more supportive neighbourhood.

I’m a bit of a maths nerd and have taught enough maths to know that without measurement, maths tells you nothing about the world. Too much economic debate now skips that step, hiding behind clever-sounding equations. What we get sounds serious, but doesn’t connect to the real economy and everyday people. As an educator my priority on Redbridge Council will be to remove the things getting in the way of you taking part in decisions. Not just because it’s good democracy, but because it’s good economics too.

Everything I do is driven by a desire to help people. I was a physics teacher until 2024, and I loved working with young people. I left the profession after seeing the lasting impact of Covid-19 on young people’s education, and feeling frustrated trying to close the attainment gap with consistent underfunding from the government.

I now work at a charity focusing on sexual violence prevention through education, visiting schools across the country and training teachers. I have previously led improvements to gender-inclusive teaching, as I firmly believe education should be accessible to all, no matter their identity or socioeconomic background. I am not afraid to challenge structures and systems to push for meaningful change.

I grew up in East London and have lived in Redbridge with my family for the last 16 years. This is my home. I see my friends, family and neighbours struggling with the cost of living, cuts to public services and poor transport links. I want to do something about this, because we all deserve better.

I care deeply about this community and believe local politics should be rooted in lived experience, not career ambition.

I have lived in Aldersbrook since 2019, moving here from Wood Street with my family, including 2 young daughters.

My decision to stand is shaped by my own experiences navigating public services and advocating for change. I have lived experience of maternal mental health challenges, which made me acutely aware of how difficult it can be to access timely and appropriate care.

I established a free support community to help mothers and birthing people navigate the healthcare system and access the support they need. This work has shown me how powerful community led solutions can be, particularly when led by people with lived experience, but also how urgently the systems need to improve so that no one falls through the gaps.

I worked for thirty years as a secondary school teacher in Redbridge. More recently I have been working with organisations that promote creativity and wellbeing through music. I’ve been working with older people, and people with a range of mental health challenges. Last year, one of the groups I work with won the Pride of Waltham Forest Cultural Connections award. Working with such a diverse range of people and facilitating their expression and fulfilment makes me feel extremely privileged and proud.

I could list many words that reflect my values. But education, creativity, care, compassion, and community are ones that come to mind right now.

I am standing for the Green Party because we oppose the policies of austerity that have had such a devastating effect on our public services. In education, for example, teachers and students face overwhelming challenges because of the negative impacts of funding cuts. With greater investment in our schools, healthcare, and essential services, we can begin to reverse the impacts of those cuts and create a fairer society in which everyone can thrive.

At a local level, The Green Party’s commitment to listening, engaging, and acting on the concerns of local people will help to make a positive change. With the guidance and support of the local community, I want to be able to work towards that change.

I believe that we don’t have to accept things as they are. We can hope for communities and a society in which our needs are met, so we can live happy, fulfilled lives.

I am a mother to 3 young children, and I am building a creative, inclusive community for the young people of Redbridge. Our workshops promote the “kids teach kids” model, giving children the opportunity to enjoy a variety of creative activities while making empowering connections. Part of my profits are regularly donated to the Redbridge food bank to help them in the fantastic work they do for the community.

I have worked for small businesses, supporting them in their operations in various capacities. I have also worked for a short period of time in the banking sector. I currently serve as a School Governor at a prestigious local grammar school and recently supported a community project that offers digital literacy provisions to residents, especially from disadvantaged backgrounds.

I have lived in Redbridge for more than 25 years, and I thoroughly understand residents’ struggles, whether that is delayed appointments, long waiting times or rising cost of living.

I stand for public consultations to be held for every important decision surrounding the community, where we build trust between the residents and the council, whether that includes the SEND provisions or more support for small local businesses.

We need a peaceful and united Wanstead Village in these divisive times. I am here to facilitate that with more accountability and transparency. We need better engagement between residents and local councillors to achieve the best for everyone.

For me, cohesion and integration are the heart of any thriving community, and since coming back from a career break after the birth of my son, I want work around multiple avenues that entirely benefit the community and open opportunities for all residents.

I have been fortunate enough to make a meaningful contribution to the local community, which has enabled me to understand where the gaps are and how we can fulfil those gaps. I am keen to actively support Wanstead Village, hence I have chosen to stand as the local council candidate because I truly believe that politics can truly begin to serve everyday people under the Green Party.
