statement_to_voters:
Hossain Tafazzal is married with two children and four grandchildren, and works at Domino's Pizza in Rugby. Hossain moved to Rugby in 2010, and for the past four years has lived in Garyth Williams Close.

He is Vice President of the Coventry Bangladesh National Party, a former Secretary of the Bangladesh Cultural Association in Rugby, and is still a member of the Association's governing committee.

Hossain has a desire to be a councillor in Rugby so that he can work for local residents and communities and strengthen the Liberal Democrat group's voice on the council.

Hossain joined the Liberal Democrats because they strongly support Britain's membership of the European Union and they believe in improving educational opportunities; they want better access to primary health care such as GP surgeries; they oppose racism; they believe in protecting and encouraging small businesses.

Hossain first worked with the Liberal Democrats when assisting Phil Bennion in his attempt to get re-elected as a Member of the European Parliament for the West Midlands in 2015. Subsequently, he has also worked with the Rugby Liberal Democrats by delivering Focus newsletters and election information and attending their meetings. In particular, he has assisted Carie-Anne Dumbleton, Bill Lewis and Glenda Allanach in the work they do in their Ward. He stood for election for the first time in New Bilton in 2016 and also then in 2017 and 2018. I stood in Newbold & Brownsover in 2019 and there were no elections in 2020.

I’m Babak (but everyone calls me Bobby). I live in Eccles and work as a policy advisor on renewable energy. I’m passionate about lowering bills for households and businesses through clean electricity and believe there is so much more we could be doing in this space locally. I am also committed to securing more social housing for Salford, and making sure new developments are genuinely shaped by local people for the benefit of current residents as well as future ones.

I spent 18 years of my life living in a run down area in the north east where the council weren’t listening and nothing was improving. It felt like we all just had to accept it. After moving to Salford four years ago, a place I’m proud to now call home, I can still see some of those same issues again. This time, I’m not accepting it. People are feeling let down and things aren’t changing. This is why I’m standing. I’m here to listen, take action and make sure things actually improve.

I'm standing as someone outside the political bubble - a working dad of two young children who cares about the kind of place families actually have to live in day to day.

I’m not here to make big promises I can’t keep, and I’m not pretending this is a safe Conservative seat. But I do think residents deserve someone willing to listen properly and speak honestly about what’s going wrong; whether that’s anti-social behaviour, poor planning decisions, or feeling like local voices are ignored.
Labour has held this area for years, and Reform will offer protest. I’m offering something simpler: a fresh set of eyes, a willingness to challenge decisions that don’t make sense, and a focus on the everyday issues people deal with on their own streets.

Even where I can’t win, I can make sure your concerns are heard - and that’s worth doing properly.

I am a local NHS community nurse and work within varied roles within the NHS, serving the whole of Salford and surrounding areas at home and in the hospital. I want to see the community that I work in thriving and making real change that is right for the people of Swinton and Wardley.

I volunteer for the charity Two Brews when I am able to fit some time in and work with them on outreach in the city centre which is something I am very passionate about.

My recent pivot in to standing as a council candidate was looking at the world, then our country and then Salford and asking how can I help make a difference? If not now, when? And if not me, then who?

As a nurse I am very adept at talking to people and explaining things with a kind and caring manner. I am confident talking to anyone, personable and relatable and know most of the families in my area either from passing them in the street or caring for them or family members.

I will work hard on local issues such as the state of our roads, crime reduction and supporting our young people. By working together to create a safer place to live, we will ensure that as a community we can all thrive.

I believe in honesty and transparency, and my life and background have given me real, practical experience of listening to people, solving problems and making the most responsible decisions in the best interests of people. The residents of Swinton and Wardley deserve a councillor who is visible, approachable and someone who is willing to stand up for their needs.

I would like the chance to be your local councillor and represent Swinton and Wardley to the best of my abilities to ensure that the Salford that we know and love is not lost. I care deeply about the people of Salford and would love the opportunity to represent your interests and needs.

I am standing as a candidate to represent Worsley & Westwood Park because I believe local voices should be heard and acted upon.

I have lived in Worsley for 20 years and have seen significant changes—from rising house prices and increasing local taxes to rapid population growth without the matching expansion in services and infrastructure.

My whole working life has been the privilege of serving the community within the NHS. As a GP in a deprived area of Bolton, I see first hand the struggles ordinary people face every day. This includes delays in GP appointments due to lack of capacity, and the challenges caused by poor coordination between GP and hospital services, which can make it harder for patients to receive timely and joined-up care.

These experiences have shaped my commitment to strengthening healthcare access and ensuring services keep pace with growing communities. I want to continue serving by helping improve the lives of local people in any way possible, working with integrity and dedication for the Worsley community.

My name is Randall, I'm a British born citizen who's also a former NHS worker as well as a survivor of a migrant attack. The United Kingdom is a Christian nation made up of England, Scotland, Wales & Ireland & I intend to fight for the right of those born here and to put the country first. I care about the safety of women, children & the innocent and their rights. I also care about the rights of veterans and pensioners as well as the right to free speech. It's not racist to put your country first. I will fight for what's right.

party_name: The Official Monster Raving Loony Party
statement_to_voters:
Friends, brothers-and-sisters-in-arms and the politically enlightened,
Do you grow tired of the fearmongering politicians hounding you to vote for them, and their boorish political party? Do you grow exasperated of the same-old same-old being spewed out of the House of Commons on a weekly basis? Are you unable to sexually satisfy your wife? If you answered yes to any of these questions, you’re in luck. We here at the glorious OFFICIAL MONSTER RAVING LOONY PARTY™ have big plans for not only this consistency of Blundellsands, but all of Britain. We are coming together; uniting both the left-wing and right-wing under one banner, and marching forward into the future. We will shut down Blundellsands Station and replace it with a Greggs, open 24/7. We will get rid of both manual AND automatic cars to make way for the return of horse-drawn carriages, solidifying our concern for the environment. Through your vote for us, you will be legally recognised as a KNIGHT OF THE LOONIES and be immediately conscripted to close-quarters combat against a polar bear, with only a water gun to defend yourself. Effective immediately. The sole survivor(s) will be graciously rewarded. Only the strong will survive and the dead will lie unburied.
In a world of boring insanity, vote for our super-sanity.

Broomhill and Sharrow Vale is my home. It is where I was raised, went to primary school and had my first job. I still live here today.

Before becoming a councillor, I did community and youth work for many years. I care deeply about these matters, especially supporting young people and families with children who have disabilities or special educational needs.

What keeps me grounded is the local community. I still volunteer in the ward and spend as much time as I can speaking to residents and helping with issues. Being a councillor is not just about meetings at the Town Hall. It is about listening, standing up for people and doing the small practical things that make our neighbourhood a better place to live.

Broomhill and Sharrow Vale shaped who I am, and it is a privilege to represent the place I call home.

Abdullah Okud — From Burngreave. For Burngreave.

Abdullah Okud is a community organiser, youth worker, trade unionist, philanthropist, and lifelong Burngreave resident. He grew up here, works here, and fights here.

With over 10 years of experience from the civil service to the voluntary sector—Abdullah has dedicated his life to serving people, helping organisations secure funding, empowering young people, and building stronger communities.

From organising Black Lives Matter protests in Sheffield to standing for Palestine and Sudan, Abdullah has never backed down from the struggle for justice. He has supported local youth clubs, grassroots football, litter campaigns, and community initiatives because real change starts on our streets.

Burngreave has been neglected for too long. Abdullah is standing as an Independent, backed by YourParty, the Muslim Vote, Vote Palestine Campaign to fight for investment, opportunity, and dignity for our people.

Real leadership. Real justice. Real change.

Vote Abdullah Okud — a voice for the community, from the community.

I’m Maia! I’m writing to ask for your support in the May council elections. I live in City Ward, in Highfield. I worked for St Mary’s Community Centre for 8½ years, have been on the local Tenants’ and Residents’ committee for 14 years and brought my children up here. In that time, I’ve run lots of projects aimed at making people’s lives better in the local community.

**Working hard all year round**

- I wrote the successful funding bid for new play equipment and landscaping of Duchess Road pocket park.
- I’ve worked on poverty, mental health awareness and youth unemployment and campaigned against disability benefit restrictions. I’ve developed and managed projects in communities across Sheffield to build community, engage volunteers and reduce food poverty.
- I am working hard to join your elected Green councillors, Douglas Johnson and Ruth Mersereau, and to follow in the footsteps of Councillor Martin Phipps. We work year-round in community groups, speak to local businesses, knock on people’s doors for a chat, and try to make everyone feel included and heard.

**Bringing back hope into politics**

Across the country, the Green Party is growing, bringing hope back into politics. Hope means moving towards a politics rooted in integrity, compassion, and action, like we’ve done in Sheffield for over 20 years now.

**This local election is about local issues and it’s between Greens and Labour here.**

Greens offer a real alternative to Labour. We’re active all year round and achieve real benefits for our residents. City Ward deserves a future shaped by the people who live here and love this area. Together, we can build it.

Ricia grew up by Crosspool’s crags with horses in fields lining lanes now absorbing Malin Bridge bottleneck-avoiding traffic, making it too dangerous to cycle, ride or walk along. 
 
 ”Motorists burn extra fuel taking circuitous routes I could safely cross to ballet school and church halls where, at 10, I saw Joe Cocker. I still support local music:  RiciaBHC Youtube shorts.” 
 
Post Notre Dame High School, Ricia graduated from Leeds University and taught at Shirecliffe College before going to Petroleum Development Oman where she became Ricia: Arabic for feather.” 
 
Then in Boston Mass. she gained an MA while working as a journalist, leading to Economist Newspaper Ltd assignments overseas making her very conscious of what draws and deters investment.  
 
“To pay for all its residents demand and politicians promise to win votes, Sheffield must be more enterprise-friendly to increase productivity per capita and ensure resources aren’t wasted. Cities need those who understand this at the helm. Lenders say no as risks of defaulting rise.” 
 
A local live music supporting landlady agrees: “Sheffield must be run like a good pub, keeping customers happy but turning a profit, otherwise there’s no money for staff.”

I'm a Mental Health Recovery Worker, founder of a local charity and passionate advocate for diversity and inclusion within the Labour party and beyond. My voluntary work with vulnerable families throughout the pandemic and the challenges I have seen firsthand across our community have motivated me to stand for councillor to make a real difference in people's lives during this difficult time.

I’ve been part of the Sheffield community for over seven years and live in Southey ward in Wadsley Bridge. It’s where I’ve lived, worked, and built my life, so it means a lot to me.

I like to stay busy, keep things simple, and focus on doing things properly. I value honesty, consistency, and just being straightforward with people.

Maz Hamilton is the Green Party candidate for Stannington Ward in the local elections on May 7th 2026

Maz Hamilton is a small business owner and recovering journalist who lives in Stannington. Their involvement in local politics stems from experiences of homelessness in youth, and from experiencing the life-changing power of well-run and generous social care, education and mental health services.

Maz is now an active fundraiser for social justice causes, including through their publishing business which supports anti-racist charities in the UK, trans rights groups and global democratic causes. They also campaign for carbon negative publishing and for small business initiatives that keep money in local economies. They are a keen walker, reader and crocheter, and have recently dug their first pond.

Cllr Wazma Qais, elected in 2019 and serving as a councillor for Silhill since then, is widely recognised for her consistent work on behalf of residents and her focus on local priorities. She has contributed to a range of improvements across Silhill, including challenging unsuitable planning applications, enhancing the Hermitage playground, supporting the repainting of the Streetsbrook Road bridge, and helping to secure the return of the Minor Injuries Unit to Solihull Hospital. She has also responded quickly to issues raised by residents, such as speeding concerns, parking problems, potholes, damaged pavements, and missed refuse collections.

As a strong voice for the community, she places residents’ concerns at the centre of her work. She is known for listening carefully, taking action promptly, and ensuring that local issues are addressed with attention to detail.

She has led local efforts to keep Silhill clean, founding the area’s first litter‑picking group and working to maintain streets free from litter and graffiti. Her commitment to community safety includes ongoing involvement in the campaign to retain Solihull Police Station.

Sardul lives in Monkspath. He was appointed as a Justice of the Peace in 1995. He is a TV presenter on the Sikh Channel.
Sardul has been involved in local politics and community affairs for over 30 years. In recognition of his services to the community, he was awarded the MBE in 1990, by Her Majesty the Queen.
He has previously served as: Labour Councillor, school governor, Chair of Police Consultative Committee, Chair of West Birmingham Health Authority, Vice-Chair of Sports Council. 
Sardul has the drive and experience to be an effective spokesperson on behalf of ALL of the people of Blythe. Time For A Change. http://www.solihulllabour.org.uk/blythe

I am an independent councillor on Shepton Mallet Town Council and we have intentionally removed destructive and polarising party politics. We now have created an environment where we respectfully debate and reach decisions.

When I look at Parliament, I see the political parties constantly at war and they do not achieve anything. If elected, I would be the first elected female independent MP in history. Pretty exciting news that we would all be a part of!

I would use this platform to unite our divided Parliament so we can treat each other with respect, value diverse points of view and reach decisions that best serve our nation as a whole.

I first fell in love with Southampton as a student, and since moving back here, I've become aware of just how much potential this city has.

Banister & Polygon is at its heart, but is being let down by substandard, overpriced housing, rubbish lining the streets, and the council's failure to think holistically about travel around the city.

As a proud trans woman, I have a history of fighting and advocating for my siblings in the LGBTQ+ community. I am standing as a council candidate to take that same energy and fight for every member of this ward and city

An active member of the community, I’m committed to Portswood, to ensure we get the clean air, well-funded care, quality housing, and healthy rivers we all deserve.

Having raised deputations at Southampton City Council questioning cuts of £39m of funds from Children and Adult Social care, and highlighting that the city is not acting on idling and air quality outside schools, I will continue holding the administration to account.

Bills continue to rise, wages stagnate,  austerity bites, with ever-increasing cuts and poor value for residents. I stand for real, practical change and hope - a break from business as usual.

I have lived in Elsworth for 7 years and have a strong sense of the importance of community life and kindness to others.

I have been politically active all my life, and am particularly passionate on issues such as equality, anti-racism, Palestine liberation, LGBTQ+ rights, and animal welfare.

I believe we need to fight to uphold our right to protest, which is currently under attack and I regularly take part in national and local demonstrations and campaigns.

For the last two years, I have also co-organised a local, fortnightly Vigil for Palestine, speaking out against our government’s complicity in the genocide of the Palestinian people.

So much is going wrong in today’s money driven society and it is imperative that we start putting people and planet over profit.

I believe, that we must tax the super rich and divert money from warfare in order to generate the funding so desperately needed for our broken public services and to bring millions of children out of poverty.

Locally, it's easy to see the need for investment in our infrastructure, the roads are littered with dangerous potholes, affordable housing is critically needed, and cheap and frequent public transport is required.

Vote Green for real hope and real change.

Hello all, I am Silke and I love living in Longstanton. But there are many things that we really need to improve: public transport, cycle paths, better lights at night, more places to meet up with lovely neighbours. We need different voices in the district council to make this all happen. The Green Party can help! We stand for social justice and a better life for all of us.

Vote Green in your local election. You have two votes, use them to make the district council more diverse, we don’t need more Lib Dem’s, we need more democracy.

If you ask why I am not campaigning for what I stand for the answer is easy. We Greens are a team, we are people powered and not supported by money from billionaires. We have limited resources and this year we are all needed to help in Histon where we have a very good chance to get in.

But still you can vote for me or my daughter who stands alongside me. People and family power!

Green wishes
Silke

RJ grew up in South Essex and works in Brentwood. As a local Healthcare Professional with over a decade of frontline service, they know how vital our public services like the NHS are. They have seen firsthand the impact of years of underfunding these critical services have taken; a decline that has accelerated since the pandemic.

RJ is a proactive member of the Green Party, contributing to the Party's Policy Development Groups on topics including Drug Reform, LGBTIQA+ rights, and Immigration and Asylum. RJ is an active volunteer in the Green Party's campaigning across South Essex, including taking a key role in the local election wins for the Greens in Southend-on-Sea.

In their own time, RJ supports Essex's vibrant grassroots music scene; a lifelong love of music, literature, and art which began at Brentwood's Hermit Youth Club, a sorely missed local facility for our young people.

I have lived in Southwark for 15 years, attended university in South London and now work here too. I have a strong and long-standing connection to this borough and care deeply about the people who call it home.  I am a passionate Liberal who believes in equality, social justice and making sure everyone feels safe and supported.
I have volunteered with Crisis at Christmas, helping the homeless, and I have worked with former Councillor Poddy Clark on community engagement and casework, listening to residents, tackling local issues and standing up for those who need help.  I have recently joined the campaign to keep the public counter at Walworth Police Station open 24/7, because accessible policing and safer neighbourhoods are essential for all of us. This work has shown me how much stronger Southwark becomes when everyone has a voice. I am standing to fight for safer streets, better housing and a fairer society. I will be visible and represent our community with honesty and hard work.

Saidat Oketunde has spent over 20 years supporting women, young people and families. As Chair of the Southwark Muslim Forum, founder of Faiths Women Community UK and a school governor, she has built local partnerships and supported residents with skills training, food security, mental health support and community safety projects.

Although I was born in Mexico and I am a proud immigrant, I have called London, especially South East London, home for the past 9 years. I want to give back to Southwark what it has given me: a home, culture, and an overall good experience. My main goal is to serve Faraday residents by understanding their experiences and listening to their feedback to identify their specific needs and concerns. I understand it will be a challenge, but I do not have any ulterior motives besides preserving the good things Faraday has and addressing its issues.

Felicia Johnston is a lifelong Southwark local who grew up in care, fuelling her passion for tackling social inequities. A project manager, she recently spearheaded a major local authority programme to drive up standards for private renters. She has also served as a school governor for six years, championing inclusion for pupils with SEND.

Sasjkia Otto moved to London aged 18, lived in a hostel, and walked between shops asking for work.

After a private sector career, she turned to Westminster to right the wrongs she’s encountered on her journey.

She’s helped develop government policy, change the law and secure investment to fix public services, build homes, and make life better for workers, disabled people and pensioners.

Cllr Portia Mwangangye says, “I may not be Southwark Born but I am definitely Southwark Bred”. From arriving as an asylum seeker to growing up across our local estates, this community has shaped her. Portia’s legal and community work led her to become a councillor, driven to serve Southwark and ensure our young people have every opportunity to thrive.

Rugiatu Kanu has a deep passion for community service. She advocates against hygiene poverty, and has restored dignity to over 2,000 women in Southwark. Rugiatu currently serves as a Service Manager in a residential home where she oversees the wellbeing and support of vulnerable adults.

Born in London, I moved back in 1979 and have lived in Southwark since 2000. A biologist and film-maker, community science projects and adult literacy coaching keep me busy in retirement. The climate and nature emergencies have such strong links with social justice that the Green Party is my natural home.

Dr Saba Manzoor is a Labour candidate rooted in North Walworth, with a British Pakistani background. An environmentalist and community organiser, she has already helped with casework on housing and benefits in the community. Saba is committed to genuinely affordable homes, thriving local high streets, cleaner air and resident-led regeneration.

Zaid Moosa works in manufacturing innovation and wants to drive Southwark council to evolve and continually improve, ensuring it delivers for everyone but especially those most in need. Zaid also wants to play his part to build a future for Southwark that has exciting opportunities and is future focused.

Marq Bailey is a local postie, trade unionist and parent, and has built an international career in film and photography. He is committed to environmental stewardship, community empowerment and delivering lasting progress for residents across Southwark today and for future generations in every local neighbourhood.

Councillor Renata Hamvas has lived in Nunhead for 27 years and was elected in 2010. She campaigns for improvements to transport, community facilities, education, and local green spaces. Renata has supported local sports and youth facilities, and work to revamp local playgrounds. She is also a governor at Kings College Hospital.

Fatmata Bah is a nurse and an academic. She lives in a council home in South Bermondsey and is currently working towards her PhD. Fatmata is proud to volunteer with Migrant Democracy Leaders and Thorowgood Community Group.

party_description_text: Independent Trade Union and Socialist Candidate

statement_to_voters: I have lived inSutton since 1978 when I moved from Wales to work in London.  I worked in the Civil Service – trade & industry – and before retiring six years ago I had spent a few years working in local government – adult social care with a focus on disability issues.  My children and grandson were born in Sutton.  Sutton has been a great place for me and my family to live and I want the same for other families. I believe that Labour will work to make this area even better.

Manish brings a strong professional background as an IT specialist with extensive experience delivering mission-critical projects across both public and private sectors. With an educational foundation that includes a degree in Engineering and an MBA, Manish is passionate about applying the same high standards to local government—ensuring services are efficient, modern, and designed around the needs of residents.
Beyond professional life, Manish has played an active role in the community for many years, as he organised resident participation in community cultural events including the High Street Christmas Light Switch-On, the Festival of Colours, and the Festival of Lights. These events have brought residents together, strengthened community bonds, and celebrated Sutton’s diversity.
Manish has lived in Sutton since 2005 and is proud to champion the Royal Marsden, Sutton United Football Club, and the borough’s exceptional schools, which contribute significantly to community life and local identity.

Priorities for Sutton and the Ward
- Transparency, Accountability, and Value for Money for Council Tax
- Revive 30 mph on main/trunk roads
- Introduce some free parking around high streets to boost small business
- Fix potholes
- Protect green spaces
- Boost cultural ecosystem in Sutton
- Boost AI education for youth in schools/colleges
- Consult on a vision for Sutton for next 5/10 years
- Review if Sutton needs more flat blocks

I was born at St Helier hospital, grew up in Wallington, and have chosen to raise my family here.

I qualified as a Chartered Accountant in public sector audit, scrutinising how councils spend public money. Over the past 15 years I’ve worked across both public and private sectors, delivering real improvements in how organisations serve the people who depend on them.

I want to bring those same skills to Wallington North, holding spending decisions to account, cutting through waste, and making sure residents get the services they pay for.

I’ve proudly lived in Swindon for 9 years after moving from West London. I work hard and love helping people. My priorities: Safe streets, reduced crime. Cutting waste to help ease the cost of living. More job and training opportunities. I will listen, act, and treat everyone with fairness and respect, regardless of background, culture or race, together we can build a better Swindon.

I grew up locally and came back in 2023, shocked by the decline. I’m standing to bring back pride, improve our town and build a Swindon that embraces its potential and offers real opportunity for everyone, not just be a commuter town with a famous roundabout.

Better Streets. Better Services.
I am standing as your Green candidate for St Andrews & Tadpole to bring real accountability to local decisions.
We need: 
More GPs, schools & local services
Safer roads & better planning
Spaces for young people
Transparent budgets - your money, your say
Vote Green on 7th May 2026
Let’s put our community first.
#VoteGreen #Swindon

I have lived in Swindon for more than 22 years, I have contributed in my own small way, by volunteering at GW Hospital, supporting the local temple & charity food run services. These experiences strengthened my belief in compassion, fairness & community responsibility. My focus is on strengthening local services, supporting families & helping build a connected community.

My name is Elmira, and I have lived in Old Town, Swindon, since 2011. I moved here and built my life here, and this community has become my home. I am a single mum of three young children, all attending local schools, so like many parents, I experience first-hand the realities of family life in Swindon.

I have watched parts of Wichelstowe grow from open fields into a thriving neighbourhood. I remember when the first buildings went up and when The Deanery School opened its doors. I have seen how decisions made locally shape our community -  for better or worse -  and I believe those decisions must always put families first.

I hold a higher degree in Psychology and Human Resources, and I work as a Technical Recruiter in a technology company. My professional background has taught me the importance of listening carefully, understanding people’s needs, and making balanced, evidence-based decisions. Every day, I work with individuals from different backgrounds and countries, which strengthens my belief in international collaboration and cooperation.

As a single parent, I understand the pressures many families face — especially with the rising cost of living. I know how difficult it can be to balance work, childcare, and financial responsibility. That is why I care deeply about practical support for families, accessible education, and strong local services.

I am delighted to stand as a candidate for the Green Party in Denton South.

Having lived in the area for over 10 years, I know how it feels to be left behind.

I have been campaigning nationally for gambling reform since 2023. In my opinion, the gambling industry is one of the most blatant displays of a transfer of wealth from the most vulnerable in our communities directly into the pockets of billionaires.

I have had first hand experience of politicians putting the interests of powerful lobby groups ahead of the people they are supposed to represent, and I am determined to fight back.

I work full time in supply chain management,  alongside my work with various gambling harm charities.  I regularly help people who have been ignored to have their voices heard, whether this is in the media or in parliament.

This is what I want to do for the people of Denton South.  For too long, our votes have been taken for granted by the Labour Party.  We proved recently that we won’t be taken for granted any more.

Vote Green in May for a Councillor who cares.

party_name: The Official Monster Raving Loony Party

statement_to_voters:
It has been an honour to serve Mossley since 2016. I am a WillowWood Patron, trustee of Home-Start-HOST, and a proud member of our Mossley community. I have worked tirelessly supporting local groups and am a strong, hardworking and reliable voice for residents.

My achievements include securing a lease for Mossley Community Centre; protecting vital green spaces such as Cosgrove Gardens; fighting Brookfields with a Woodland Protection Order; challenging unsuitable developments on Hanover Street, Andrew Street, Stamford Road and Woodend View. I continue to support residents on Hey Farm, backed Article 4 rules strengthening oversight of HMOs, implemented two hours free parking, improvements to parks, public spaces and street furniture, and helped launch our award-winning Mossley Farmers Market.

I will continue: 
* supporting residents & community groups
* protecting Mossley’s character
* growing a vibrant cultural offer for everyone locally

I’m proud to stand again for Mossley.

I've lived in Tamworth my whole life. I was raised and schooled in Stonydelph, and have called Belgrave home for the past 15 years.

Belgrave has been forgotten about. During my time living in the ward I've seen green spaces become unruly, community buildings become dilapidated and unusable, and important transport routes pretty much fall apart. I take pride in my home and I want to make sure we get our fair share of council funding to fix these issues.

My campaign policies are all about designing community-focused solutions that work for all residents within the ward and across the wider Tamworth borough.

I don't have the space to list them here, so head to facebook.com/greensforbelgrave to check them out!

I'm standing for the Green Party because I believe that the climate crisis, cost-of-living crisis, and societal unrest we're seeing are all part of the same problem. You can't solve one without tackling the other, and the Greens are the only party advocating for that!

To summarise, I'm a lifetime local representative with genuine compassion for the ward I'm standing in. I'll be a strong advocate for Belgrave, and I can't wait to help.

Please get in touch via greensforbelgrave@outlook.com or my social media channels to find out more 💚

Vikki has served as a councillor for Chafford
Hundred for three years and is a long-term
local resident whose children went to school
in the area.
Vikki is known for being responsive and
hardworking, representing residents’ concerns,
supporting local services and helping keep
Chafford Hundred a great place to live.

Elena PELIN and Qaisar ABBAS are Conservative Councillor Candidates for West Thurrock and South Stifford Ward. Qaisar is your local Councillor with a great track record of delivering projects. With Qaisar’s experience and Elena’s fresh perspective, they will work hard to improve Local Services. Qaisar and Elena will always be ready to listen, respond and act on what matter most to you and ensure that improvements benefit everyone in this area and no one is left behind. This is Local Election and both live locally in West Thurrock and South Stifford Ward.

Elena and Qaisar's leaflets can be viewed at www.facebook.com/qaisarabbasuk

Both candidates are requesting residents of West Thurrock and South Stifford to VOTE for them on 7th May 2026. The Polls will open from 7am till 10pm and voters need Photo ID to cast their vote.

A City professional and proud Limehouse resident, Jurgen is committed to our community. He is campaigning for stronger tenants’ rights and the creation of welcoming third spaces where neighbours can connect. Jurgen is dedicated to making our streets and parks safer. He is looking to build a strong sense of community cohesion. He believes everyone should be able to walk in their street and feel safe, happy, and energised.

His name truly reflects his character. Amin Rahman.
Alongside his roles as a school governor and a college lecturer, Amin remains committed to public service. He is ready to take on new responsibilities and serve the community with honesty, integrity, respect and care.
He is a retired Wing Commander equivalent to a Lieutenant Colonel from the Bangladesh Air Force and later worked as an airline pilot and in senior management roles.
Amin would be honoured to have your support.

Syeda Bushra Raza brings energy, empathy, and real-world experience to her candidacy for the Stretford and Humphrey Park ward in the 2026 local elections to Trafford Council. A proud mother of two, Syesa understands the everyday challenges facing families and is passionate about making a meaningful difference in the lives of local residents.

With a professional background in banking and customer service, Syeda has developed strong communication and problem-solving skills—qualities that make her an effective advocate for the community. Her frontline experience gives her insight into the issues that matter most to residents, from safety and support to dignity and opportunity.

Syeda’s priorities are clear:

•     Tackling crime through stronger community policing and practical prevention measures

•     Supporting the elderly and vulnerable, ensuring care and respect for those most in need

She is committed to building safer neighbourhoods and standing up for those who often go unheard. Compassionate, determined, and deeply rooted in the community, Syeda Bushra Raza is ready to be a strong local voice for Stretford.

Ali Aydogdu at 21 years old is the 2026 Local Conservative Councillor candidate for the Urmston ward. Studying at Trafford college, Altrincham campus.

As a resident of Urmston I strive to improve and better our area through good communication with residents. The views and opinions of the Urmston residents is what matters the most. Ever since I joined the North Trafford Conservatives I have seen the strive and determination of our team to reach our goals and our ideal image of the area we care about. I am optimistic about the future and I look forward to helping the residents in making our area the best it can be.

As a Brownhills councillor, I will fight for there to be a local police office open to the public where police officers can be deployed from for the local area. Creating swifter responses and police presence to deter anti social behaviour and theft. I will work to use funding for ravens court and other run down and closed off buildings in our area. We want to bring back a market for our visitors and locals for choice and vibrancy. We would like community centres for our young and old to use for projects and activities. Let’s bring safety and pride back to our Brownhills; enabling it to thrive once more.

Akhter Ali and Jamal Ahmed are ‘Independent Candidates’
They are free from Party affiliation and restrictions.They can advocate for their ward and constituents, speak up for people regardless of race, religion and ethnicity, without constraints.

Their sole aim is the rejuvenation of their ward and greater Walsall area.

Their loyalty will be to the people, not to political parties 
or any party agendas.

**About Kris Jack**
Kris lives in Waltham Forest with his husband and two sons. He’s a business owner and entrepreneur who co-founded the UK’s largest mental wellness app for the LGBTQ+ community.

After a PhD in AI, he moved into industry to build things that help real people. He’s co-chaired his school PTA, navigated the SEND system for his boys, and worked with the council to solve problems on his street.

**What Kris Stands For**
I’m standing because people in Chingford Green deserve councillors who actually listen and do the work. Too many people in our communities feel unseen and unsupported.

That’s not good enough.

I believe in working hard, looking out for each other, and helping those who need it. I want more green spaces, stronger communities and housing my children will afford.

**About Yuksel Gonul**
I have lived in the borough for over twenty years. I work as a finance coordinator in enviromental management. I bring extensive experience in forecasting, budgeting, and operational efficiency.

My focus is to create cleaner and safer streets in the neighbourhood and improving the rate of recycling to support a greener and fairer future. Beyond my professional role, I am a member of the Kurdish community and a human rights campaigner.

I am commited to addressing local neighbourhood issues and advocating for the well-being of our community.

**What Yuksel Stands For**
I deeply care about our borough and our community and want to contribute to it. I am standing to challenge the current local administration because I want this borough to change for the better. I want to see cleaner and safer streets, and more affordable and social housing.

I also want to see the protection of green environment which is the lifeline of the community. I am for the lower bills, which will help residents who struggles to pay the bills. More importantly I want to ensure the residents’ voices are heard and that they are part of the decision-making process.

I believe in a greener borough and I am looking to bring the change to the area.

**About Simone McNichols-Thomas**
I was born in Hackney, but grew up in Oxford – I made my way back to London over 10 years ago and when I moved to Waltham Forest I quickly grew to love its energy and diversity. With a professional background in communications in the Higher Education sector, I care about amplifying important stories and ensuring people have a say in policy change that affects them.

As a second-generation immigrant and someone who belongs to other marginalised communities, I’m passionate about social justice and believe that local government should work for everyone – not just those who already have influence.

If elected, I will work hard to ensure Forest Ward is a place where residents feel heard – regardless of if they voted for me or not.

**What Simone stands for**
I can see divisive politics and austerity measures eroding the foundations of community. That’s why I’m committed to rebuilding forgotten community spaces and supporting grassroots organising.

I’m fortunate to be a recent homeowner and think it’s a basic right to have a secure and affordable home, irrespective of whether you rent or own. I’m also advocating for consistent management of green spaces and accessible, safe walking routes. These should be a right of passage where we live and raise our families.

In my opinion, London is the best city in the world – but I know it can only thrive if it is fair, green and affordable for all.

**About Lukas Simma**
Hi, I’m Lukas. I moved to Waltham Forest after my studies in 2022 to start my career in London, and have since fallen completely in love with this amazing corner of London.

I’m an audio engineer working in the post production industry, and experience the struggles of young professionals in London day to day, from cost of living, high grocery prices, underwhelming quality of housing stock, to crumbling infrastructure and lacking public services.

I hope to bring positive change to Waltham Forest as a Green Party councillor.

**What Lukas stands for**
I joined the Green Party because they were the only party with a real, genuinely new approach to politics.

Not every car park has to be for profit, not every park has to be neglected, not every new housing has to be built for rent only. It’s time to finally stop running the council for profit, and start running it for its residents, regardless of gender, ethnicity, wealth, religion, heritage, sexual orientation, or disability.

What has been lacking in this country was not the knowledge of what to do differently, but the will to do those things differently.

**About Em Dean**
I moved to London in 2016, and I quickly fell in love with this city! I’m a university lecturer in computer science. In politics, I’ve been involved in various policy construction, mostly in education and equalities issues, and some of my policies have become law or official guidance in Wales, Scotland, and overseas.

I also have some expertise in electoral compliance and regulation, and internal party governance. It’s very important to me that political parties work to elect responsible, ethical representatives who put local people first.

**What Em stands for**
I’m standing for the Green Party because I think voters deserve councillors that work for voters, not for themselves or special interests. By electing Greens we are electing a strong and passionate voice for local people and local interests.

**About Marco Hacon**
I’ve lived in London for the past 10 years and have long been connected to Waltham Forest through regular visits to family. Having recently made the area my home, I’m committed to being an active voice for local residents.

I have a Masters’ in Environment, Politics, and development, and have dedicated the last 5 years of my career to tackling climate change.

In my spare time I like to participate in triathlons as well as volunteer for green and social issues.

**What Marco stands for**
I stand for putting people and planet first. That means tackling the climate crisis locally through cleaner air, better green spaces, and sustainable transport. I believe politics should serve communities with decisions shaped by local voices that ensure no one is left behind.

I’m committed to fairness, wellbeing, and practical action that improves everyday life. Ultimately, I’d like to see a more inclusive and resilient Waltham Forest.

**About Ijaz Kato**
Walthamstow for me has always been my anchor for who I am. Having been born in Whipps Cross, I’ve lived in D.C., Singapore and Bristol before coming back permanently to Walthamstow and have lived here for 5 years. I’ve always travelled back to Walthamstow as I’ve seen it as my home, and want to do what I feel it’s done for me for most of my life, make Walthamstow a home for everyone.

I work in the Film and TV industry as a Production Co-ordinator and run my own film distribution company! I also help volunteer at Rukshana Khan Foundation.

**What Ijaz stands for**
For me, the biggest issues are in relation to accessibility, in terms of disabled access along with accessibility to services like garbage cleaning. Creating green spaces, clearer air and effictive services for those who need it.

Flytipping especially is an issue that I feel needs to be solved to make the streets a more welcoming and habitable place. Along with this, as a filmmaker, providing artistic opportunities for young people can provide a great outlet not currently being provided to children.

**About Hannaan Malik**
Hannaan is a proud Walthamstow local, born and raised here, and now standing as the Green Party candidate for Hoe Street ward. Having grown up in the area and attended local schools, he understands first-hand the pressures residents face, from the cost of living to housing, cleaner streets and safer neighbourhoods.

As a foster carer, Hannaan has spent years supporting vulnerable young people and families, offering stability, care and practical help when it is needed most. This experience has shaped his belief that strong communities are built on compassion, fairness and looking out for one another.

**What Hannaan stands for**
Hannaan is standing to give Hoe Street a fresh, community-focused voice, one that listens, acts and puts residents first. He is committed to making the area greener, safer and more affordable, while protecting the sense of community that makes Walthamstow such a special place to live.

**About Solene**

I have lived and worked in East London for over 17 years and have long been vocal in campaigning with and on behalf of local resident groups. I am not a career politician, but a local and outspoken resident who has grown increasingly frustrated with party politicians who fail to attend community meetings, respond to emails or properly represent the people they were elected to serve. I believe I can do a better job of addressing residents’ concerns than the current Labour councillors, whom many feel are largely absent.

I care deeply about my neighbourhood and the diverse community that calls it home. I see serious issues being overlooked due to weak representation: a lack of meaningful engagement, overstretched GP services, large private-sector tower blocks, neglect of nature and green spaces, dangerously high air pollution, unsafe playgrounds and a shortage of spaces for young people. At the same time, the council continues to approve major unaffordable developments that will dramatically increase the ward’s population, without strengthening the already strained public services serving Lea Bridge.

**What Solene stands for**

Residents are being ignored.

Parents don’t even try calling the GP anymore — it’s overwhelmed and impossible to reach — so they’re forced to take their children straight to A&E.

Neighbours are worried about more than 10 tower blocks being built on contaminated Gasworks land, over six years and within the same half mile. Neighbouring 3 schools including 2 nurseries and no guarantees on monitoring air quality and toxic dust on and in homes.

Our green spaces are essential. They protect our health, preserve wildlife, clean polluted air, absorb heavy rainfall and help safeguard us on a flood plain. Building over them puts our future at risk.

The council appears quicker to cooperate with developers than to stand up for residents’ interests.

Poor transport planning is damaging daily life. We’ve lost a key bus route, there’s no service down Orient Way, trains are unreliable, and rushed LTNs and poorly designed cycle lanes are creating congestion and pollution. Local residents know these streets best — they must be at the heart of these decisions.

Meanwhile, community buildings sit closed and neglected. Jubilee Park Pavilion should reopen to serve all ages and abilities. The Waterworks Centre should once again be the vibrant educational hub and café our community deserves.

I am standing for election in Leyton because working-class families in our community are being pushed to the breaking point. We are being priced out of our own neighbourhoods by skyrocketing, unaffordable housing, squeezed by low pay and a lack of secure work, and abandoned by devastating local cuts to the essential council services we rely on every day. It is time to fight back against austerity and demand a council that puts the needs of ordinary people before profit.

​Cut Rents

Restore Jobs

Restore Services

**About Demetrius Williams**
I’m standing to be a Green Party councillor because our borough deserves councillors who are visible, honest and focused on what matters to residents.

As a local campaigner and Waltham Forest resident, I’ve worked with a number of local community groups that amplify underrepresented voices in the borough. This includes Waltham Forest for a Free Palestine, which is currently working to hold the council accountable to its pledge to divest its pension investments from companies complicit or linked to war crimes.

**What Demetrius stands for**
My priorities include:

_Housing and Green Spaces_
Push for genuinely affordable, high-quality homes that meet environmental standards and hold private developers to account. Protect our green spaces and preserve their biodiversity and community life.

_Health and Social Care_
Promote accessible, quality healthcare (especially for vulnerable residents) and strengthen local social care support.

_Safer, Cleaner Streets_
Tackle pollution, improve safety and keep our streets and public spaces clean, inclusive and well-maintained.

_Fair Local Economy_
Back small businesses and community groups that ensure support reaches those who need it most.

_Community Solidarity and Ethics_
Stand up for fairness, equality and environmental justice to ensure our borough acts with compassion.

I’ll put our community first. I’ll challenge complacency and lead with the energy, compassion and empathy needed to serve Leyton.

favourite_biscuit: Got to be a classic chocolate chip cookie!
statement_to_voters:
**About Chanté Johnson**
My name is Chante, I am a daughter, sister, very proud auntie and loyal friend, London has been a constant in my life and my love for its ecosystem has only grown, the ability to find community no matter who you are or what you’re looking for, you can find it here.

I have worked service jobs to remote jobs making me no stranger to hard work. Politics has been another constant as a young black woman it seems intrinsically linked to my identity leading me to write my university dissertation on black hair and all that it means. Seeking out opportunities such as volunteering for organisations such as our streets now, even going vegan.

I am always looking for ways to better, make a difference and most importantly to help.

**What Chanté stands for**
I am standing for Markhouse because this is how we make the positive change we desperately need. It’s normal everyday people stepping forward and stepping up to help the local community in whichever way we can. I want to speak up for people who no longer feel heard, to put the needs of my community first. I can use my knowledge and capabilities to fight for what is right and what is fair, to challenge and change the status quo that keeps our cost of living far too high.

I have (as I suspect we all have) watched from the sidelines and felt the hopelessness at watching our country go down a path of decisiveness and what seems like never ending crisis. So, I am saying let’s all step forward and step up and choose hope, lets elect Greens! and create the change we want to see.

**About Rbeeza Mobeen**
My name is Rbeeza, and I’ve lived in Walthamstow since primary school, so a lot of my formative upbringing was around these parts!

I’m now a social impact reporter for a fabulous little charity that combines art and activism to bring about social change, and love bringing my artsy expertise and lived experience as a first-gen migrant to our workshops and projects. You’ll probably find me at the libraries checking out a gazillion books (or asking for info on how to book the space for an event) or at the wetlands, doing a walk and talk with local charities.

I’m always looking to uplift the most marginalised voices in our community, and learning from those who came before me in the fights for justice.

**What Rbeeza stands for**
As a first-generation immigrant, I’ve seen what making non-abolitionist local reforms does to strengthen the existing social inequality in an area.

I stand for the populations residing in Waltham Forest: our communities, our livelihoods, and our chances at succeeding in life should be made easier and more accessible, rather than prioritising vacant luxury flats that none of us can afford.

I stand for the families in Waltham Forest whose politics are unheard and cast aside, and who feel that they’re being pushed to the sidelines as a result of ever-increasing socio-economic policies.

**About Samir Richards**
Delighted to be an adopted member of the Walthamstow Community. I moved to Walthamstow from Luton in 2022 and have loved every second living here. I am an Assistant Principal at a high performing school locally.

I have immersed myself into the borough and you may have seen me at Walthamstow FC (or Arsenal), cycling along the canal or spending an afternoon at any of the many breweries, though the Flower Pot remains the favourite. I have two (black and white and very unusual looking for a labradoodle) labradoodles that I walk regularly at Hollow Pond and Lloyd Park on the weekend.

**What Samir stands for**
Social justice – I’ve worked in schools for nearly 10 years now and I’ve seen the difference we can make on a daily basis. I hope that working in this capacity means I can do more to support even more people.

Local councils affect people’s lives on a day-to-day basis and I think we can do better. I want a greener, more affordable Walthamstow that benefits everyone.

I am passionate about a shift to bold, truthful & compassionate politics that puts quality of life for everyone as the top priority. Where change isn't dismissed as "too hard" & where I'm empowered to make the world a better place for my children.

As a mother of two little boys, Yannina is appalled by the state of things in England and abroad. She understands that in a world that is interconnected, global security and national security go hand in hand. Her priority as councillor would be to empower local communities and build climate resilience whilst tackling deep inequality.

I lived in Tooting Bec for nearly 10 years. I am fluent in Urdu/Hindi, Punjabi, and Pashto, with functional knowledge of Arabic and Persian. I am an advocate of the High Court and a lawyer in Pakistan, recently called to the Bar at Middle Temple as a Barrister. I support charity work as a legal caseworker or paralegal, including Bail for Immigration Detainees, handling bail applications for immigration detainees. As a candidate for Tooting Bec councillor, I strongly support more social housing, free education, free school meals, additional playgrounds and sports facilities for youth, and the promotion of local small businesses. I would like the council to invest in leisure centres, safe cycling routes, benches for adults and pregnant women, and increased CCTV for safety, along with strict measures against shoplifting and violence targeting children and women. If elected, my priorities include fostering integration, promoting diversity, and ensuring a safe and happy community.

**It is my pleasure to represent Welham Green and Hatfield South at Welwyn Hatfield Borough Council, Hatfield South at Hatfield Town Council and as the Executive member for Environment at Welwyn Hatfield Borough Council and Deputy leader of the Council.**

Since being elected in 2022, I’ve been working hard to be a voice for the community both in and out of of the Council chamber. Welham Green and Hatfield South is a diverse community with many concerns and aspirations for what they want from their Council. Me and my colleagues Paul and Adrienne are doing what we can to make Welham Green and Hatfield South the best place to live and work. This involves dealing with issues that affect our residents. Including Parking, Fly tipping and maintaining our Green spaces.

In May 2023, I was given the privilege of becoming the Executive member for Environment. This has given us the opportunity to fix the appalling state of grass cutting in our borough, neglected by the previous Conservative administration. My other priorities for this position will be maintaining a high quality of waste collection both residentially and at recycling centres as well as combating fly tipping.

As Welwyn Hatfield Borough Council’s youngest Councillor as well as a former student of the University of Hertfordshire, I do my best to represent members of the community that often feel neglected by modern Politics. I also run the Welham Green friendship group, a monthly get together setting to combat isolation in older members of our community, which is even more important in a post-Covid world.

People in Westminster deserve a council that works well, listens carefully, and gets the basics right.

In Church Street, residents have told us they want to feel safer, see cleaner streets, and know that their local area is being looked after properly. They want services that work, spending that makes sense, and a council that pays attention to what local people are saying.

That is what we want to help deliver.

We are standing in May because we believe Westminster should be a place where people feel proud of their neighbourhood, confident in their streets, and clear that their concerns are being heard.

Our priorities are simple: safer streets, cleaner public spaces, and better value for local taxpayers.

We want to bring a more practical, responsive approach to Westminster Council, focused on the things that matter most to residents.

On Thursday 7th May, you have the chance to choose the future direction of Westminster. We would be grateful for your support.

Natalia Awasty, Ahmed Imtiaz, and Magdalena Krzyzanowska
Your local Conservative candidates for Westminster's Church Street ward

Zubin has lived a few hundred metres from Church Street for his whole life, and has always loved the vibrancy of the area and its many food stalls, shops, and arts spaces. He joined the Green Party because of its support for environmental protection and immigrants’ rights, and its approach to reducing socio-economic inequality. Having helped to deliver sporting and academic youth programmes for several years, he is particularly keen to ensure that young people in Church Street have opportunities to develop their skills and engage with their community, which can also help to reduce rates of anti-social behaviour and crime.

People in Westminster deserve a council that works well, listens carefully, and gets the basics right.

In Church Street, residents have told us they want to feel safer, see cleaner streets, and know that their local area is being looked after properly. They want services that work, spending that makes sense, and a council that pays attention to what local people are saying.

Natalia Awasty, Ahmed Imtiaz, and Magdalena Krzyzanowska
Your local Conservative candidates for Westminster's Church Street ward

From the Harrow Road corridor to the wider neighbourhood, residents deserve streets that feel safe, clean, and well looked after.

People here know the importance of strong local services, reliable street cleaning, and a council that pays attention to the realities of daily life. Whether it is around the Grand Union Canal, local shopping parades, or the homes and estates that make up the ward, residents want to feel that their area is being properly cared for.

That is the standard we want to bring.

We believe Westminster should be a place where local people are listened to, public spaces are kept in good order, and taxpayers’ money is spent wisely.

Our focus is on the basics that matter most: safe streets, clean neighbourhoods, and better value for money.

On Thursday 7th May, you have the chance to back a more practical approach for Harrow Road and for Westminster.

Alexander Hawthorn, Iheoma Oteh, and Samuel Parr
Your local Conservative candidates for Westminster's Harrow Road ward

1. Crime and ASB: “What will you do to address crime and deal with anti-social behaviour on the Hyde Park Estate? Do you have any plans for increased CCTV coverage?”

Westminster Council plans to install 350 new CCTV cameras by 2030 and we would work alongside the council to ensure this happens. 200 have already been delivered. 
Residents tell us that the biggest issue is not the lack of CCTV but the lack of follow‑up when problems are reported. We want the Council to work with the police so residents actually get responses, not reference numbers.
We support practical measures that make people feel safe, including visible community policing.
We also want investment in youth services and community programmes, because prevention is always more effective than enforcement alone.

Please see below an example of a Green Party Councillor tackling crime in Bristol:

In 2023, Green councillor Mohamed Makawi, whose friend had been fatally stabbed in Castle Park, proposed a motion to bring down knife crime in Bristol that was passed by full Council.

Rather than simply calling for more policing, the Green motion outlined practical solutions and addressed the social factors underlying knife violence.

It introduced simple but vital steps to save lives, such as providing emergency bleed kits and staff training for night-time venues, and providing more lighting and CCTV to fix ‘blind spots’ in areas of high knife crime.

2. Planning (double-glazing): “Many houses on the Hyde Park Estate are unable to install double-glazing due to planning constraints. This results in increased noise pollution and poor energy efficiency. What can you do rectify or mitigate this?”

We want planning rules to allow residents to live in warm, quiet, energy‑efficient homes.
We will push the Council to adopt modern, conservation‑grade double‑glazing and secondary‑glazing solutions that are already permitted in other conservation areas.
Improving insulation and reducing energy waste is essential for both climate action and household bills  and we believe the Council should enable it, not block it.

I personally have also noticed an increase in noise pollution since Labour took over Westminster council. It seems a lot more construction is being allowed and whilst I appreciate this grows the economy, it feels as if residents’ needs have been neglected. I resorted to installing secondary glazing in my home after having a miserably loud building extension permitted directly opposite me. Westminster council needs reminding that people live here.

3. Airbnb: “Accommodation that is short-let illegally can cause untold misery to residents. What will you do to control Airbnbs, and ensure if they have approval that they also show appropriate insurance and pay taxes?”

Westminster Council currently has 2500 cases of illegal subletting open for investigation. This is clearly a problem that overwhelms the council and needs more staffing, which we would push for.
We will push for proactive enforcement not just responding to complaints so residents aren’t left to police the problem themselves.
Short‑lets must not undermine community stability or reduce the supply of homes for people who actually live here.
We support strict enforcement of the 90‑day rule and stronger action against illegal short‑lets that cause noise, security issues, and loss of long‑term housing.
We want the Council to require registration of all short‑let properties, with proof of insurance, safety compliance, and tax obligations.

4. Traffic: “We nearly had a low traffic neighbourhood several years ago, but instead the Council put in place measures that failed adequately to address the traffic issues. What would you do to deal with the traffic?”

We want a fresh, resident‑led traffic plan for the Hyde Park Estate that actually reduces through‑traffic rather than shifting it from one street to another.
Any scheme must be co‑designed with residents, businesses and emergency services not imposed from above.
We support measures that cut rat‑running, improve crossings, and make streets safer for walking and cycling, while protecting access for disabled people and essential vehicles.
We would also push for better enforcement of speeding, idling and illegal manoeuvres, which residents consistently raise as daily problems.
The goal is simple: quieter, safer streets where people can move around without constant congestion and pollution.

The people of Westminster deserve the best from their council.

But people across Bayswater and Lancaster Gate have told us that they do not feel as safe as they used to. Streets are not as clean as they should be. Costs are rising while standards fall. And too often, it feels like the Labour-led council is simply not listening.

That must change. And you can help change it.

We are standing as your local Conservative candidates in May because Westminster deserves a council that keeps your city safe, looks after your streets, spends your money wisely, and listens to local people.

Our plan has been shaped by what you have told us matters most: safe streets, clean public spaces, and your money spent wisely.

No more excuses, waste, or reckless spending.

We have the plan, the team and the commitment to deliver it. But we need your support to make it happen.

On Thursday 7th May, you have the chance to vote for real change in Westminster. We are asking for your trust and your vote to deliver the Westminster you deserve.

Jonathan Goff, Blessings Kaseke and Philip Stephenson-Oliver
Your local Conservative candidates for Westminster's Lancaster Gate ward

Born and raised locally, I went to Hallfield Primary School. I am currently running an oral history project to record and preserve the stories of local people. I’m interested in improving the public realm, the ongoing Queensway development, community provision, affordable housing and fair rent.

On 7th May you will have a choice about the future of Pimlico. Continued neglect under Labour, or action with the Conservatives.

We’ve all seen Pimlico deteriorate. The streets are messier, crime is up, our tube station is often broken or shut, Pimlico Proms cancelled, and repairs take forever. We’ve also seen a lack of investment into St George’s Square Dog Park and Gardens, and a failure to deliver the renovation of Pimlico Gardens.

This is before you get to the decade of destruction set to be caused by Labour’s plan for the Pimlico District Heating Undertaking (PDHU).

It doesn’t have to be like this, and the Conservatives have a plan to get the Council working for you again.

We will invest in more street cleaning and create a Cabinet Member for Enforcement to ensure those who break the rules are caught. We will actually stand up to Sadiq Khan over crime and cuts to our transport links. We will fund and restore the Pimlico Proms. We will deliver the improvements to Pimlico Gardens, and we will fix the dog park.

Finally, we will scrap Labour’s PDHU plans, go back to the drawing board, consult properly with residents, and implement a block-by-block approach.

Recent polls show it is going to be really close here in Pimlico South, meaning if the Conservatives win here, we will most likely win back the council. So, on May 7th, the power really is in your hands.

Vote Conservative and Protect Pimlico.

Murad Gassanly, Roberto Salvia, and James Spencer
Your local Conservative candidates for Westminster's Pimlico South ward

On 7th May you will have a choice about the future of Pimlico. Continued neglect under Labour, or action with the Conservatives.

We’ve all seen Pimlico deteriorate. The streets are messier, crime is up, our tube station is often broken or shut, Pimlico Proms cancelled, and repairs take forever.
